FullText: » Do not pray like the hypocrites ! They want everyone to see them in the houses of worship and on the street corners . I assure you they have been paid in full .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'paid' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (implied agent/payer) and an internal argument (they/hypocrites as patient/theme receiving payment), construed as directed from payer toward the recipients and affecting them.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have been paid' is passive and licenses an external argument (the hypocrites as agent), so it does not meet the criterion of encoding a one-participant event with only an internal argument and no external argument.

AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have been paid in full' instantiates AktAcc as it describes a dynamic process of payment that unfolds over time and culminates in a defined terminal point (the completion of full payment), with an inherent process phase leading toward that completion state.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have been paid' describes a resultant state of completion rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it focuses on the achieved condition (being paid) rather than the instantaneous threshold-crossing moment of payment itself.

AktState_explanation: The target 'have been paid' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (receiving payment in full), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.

DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have been paid in full' asserts a resulting state (they currently possess their reward/payment) rather than merely recording whether the event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'have been paid' indicates a change of state from unpaid to paid, with the resulting state (being paid in full) holding at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have been paid' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, making it present perfect aspect rather than durative universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been paid' presents the payment as a bounded, completed event viewed as a whole from the present moment, with the internal temporal development of the paying process not made accessible, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.

FullText: » When you abstain from food , do not be like the hypocrites , with sad faces to be seen by men . They have received their reward .

AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have received' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the moment of receiving the reward), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'They have received their reward' describes an actual state of affairs in the world presented by the text, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.

DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have received' presents the event of receiving a reward as part of the hypocrites' life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have received' indicates a change of state from not having received to having received a reward, with the resulting state (possession of reward) holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' expresses a state (possession of reward) that began in the past and continues to hold at the reference time, instantiating the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' presents the reward-receiving as a completed, bounded whole event with relevance to the present moment, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'They have received their reward' asserts a realized fact about the hypocrites' actual condition, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: When Jesus had finished these words , the crowds were astonished at what he taught .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'finished' licenses an internal argument ('these words'), so it does not encode a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had finished' describes a dynamic, durative event (the speaking of words) that necessarily culminates in a defined terminal point (completion of speaking), with an inherent process phase leading toward that completion.
AktAch_explanation: The pluperfect 'had finished' marks the punctual boundary point where Jesus crossed the threshold from speaking to having completed speech, representing a definable change-of-state transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: "had finished" denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint and result state, not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: "had finished" entails a resulting change of state (completion of speaking), which violates the AktSemel requirement that there be no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had finished' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint, involving dynamic change and progression, which contradicts the AktState feature requiring a situation that holds over time without change or inherent endpoint.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When Jesus had finished these words' describes a completed action that actually occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had finished' presents the event of Jesus finishing his words as a realized, completed action in the past, not as absent from reality or not occurring.

DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had finished' presents the completion of Jesus speaking as an event in his life history that occurred at least once, answering whether the event happened without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had finished' indicates a completed action prior to the reference time (when crowds were astonished), not a change of state with the resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had finished' expresses a completed action prior to another past event (the crowds being astonished), not a continuous state from past to reference time as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had finished' presents the completion of speaking as a bounded, single event without internal temporal development, meeting the TLAspPfv criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had finished' is a past perfect form asserting a realized, actual event (Jesus completing his words), not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had finished' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect tense (pluperfect), which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.

FullText: When Jesus heard it , he was amazed , and said to the crowd : » I tell you , I have not found such great faith in Israel .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'found' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (I/agent) and an internal argument (such great faith/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the agent toward the theme, satisfying the ASTrns feature.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have not found' entails a resulting change of state (discovery/knowledge acquisition) and describes a durative search experience rather than a punctual, instantaneous event.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have not found' presents the event of finding such great faith as not occurring or not realized in Israel, instantiating ClNegPol.

DtRes_explanation: The gram 'have not found' encodes a transition from a state of searching/not having found to a resultant state where the absence of finding holds at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have not found' expresses a negative state (not finding) that extends continuously from a past starting point (Jesus's life/experience) up to the reference time (the moment of speaking), instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have not found' presents a state of non-discovery extending from past to present rather than a bounded completed transition, failing to meet the TLAspPfv requirement of construing the situation as a bounded whole with the transition itself as a single completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have not found' asserts a realized fact about Jesus's actual experience searching through Israel, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: They screamed at him , saying : » What have we to do with you , Son of God ? Have you come to torment us before the time ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' is intransitive and encodes motion/arrival with only an external argument (you), lacking the two-participant transitive structure with an internal affected argument required by ASTrns.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have you come' expresses a resultant state (arrival at a location with purpose to torment), not a punctual dynamic event without resulting change of state, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have you come' is a perfective construction indicating a completed action with a definite endpoint, not a state that holds over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Have you come to torment us before the time?' is a direct question about the addressee's actual purpose/intention, not a counterfactual statement presenting a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have you come' is a yes/no question about whether an event occurred, not a negation or presentation of an event as absent from reality.

DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have you come' is a present perfect question asking about a completed action at an unspecified past time, not expressing a state or activity holding continuously from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have you come' presents the action of coming as a completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition as a bounded whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The interrogative 'Have you come to torment us before the time?' construes the situation as hypothetical and non-actual, expressing uncertainty about the purpose of the addressee's arrival rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have you come' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have you come' is an active voice construction where 'you' is the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of coming, not a patient receiving an action, and there is no passive morphological marking.

FullText: But when the people saw it , they marveled , and glorified God who had given such authority to men .

DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had given' expresses a completed past action (God gave authority) prior to the reference time (when people marveled), not a state or activity holding continuously from past to present.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had given' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact (God's actual granting of authority), not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had given' is a past perfect form that encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.

FullText: Jesus turned to see her and said : » Daughter , take courage ; your faith has made you well . « The woman was healed at that time .

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this in Israel' is a factual statement about past experience, not a counterfactual condition; it asserts what has not occurred in reality rather than describing a situation contrary to fact under different conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The phrase 'have never seen' presents the event of seeing such a thing as not occurring in the past experience of the crowd, construing the situation as absent from their reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'We have never seen anything like this in Israel' contains 'never' which is a negation adverb, not an overt temporal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have never seen' presents the event of seeing as part of the crowd's life history, answering whether they have experienced this phenomenon at least once (they have not), without asserting any current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have never seen' encodes a transition from a state of not having experienced something to the current state of never having experienced it, with this negative resultant state holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have never seen' expresses a negative state (not having witnessed something) that holds continuously from the past up to the reference time of speaking, matching the DtUniv feature definition.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have never seen' asserts a realized fact about the crowd's past experience (what they have actually witnessed), not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have never seen' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have never seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'we' as the semantic agent performing the action of seeing, not a patient receiving the action.

FullText: » When they persecute you in this city , flee to the next . I tell you ; before you have gone through all the cities of Israel the Son of man will come .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'gone through' encodes a two-participant event with an implicit external argument (you) as agent and 'all the cities of Israel' as internal argument (patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the agent toward and affecting the theme through traversal.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb phrase 'have gone through' encodes a two-participant event where 'you' (external argument/agent) actively traverses 'all the cities of Israel' (internal argument/theme), thus licensing an external argument and failing to meet the one-participant, internal-argument-only criterion for ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'have gone through' licenses an internal argument ('all the cities of Israel'), violating the one-participant external-argument-only requirement of ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' describes a dynamic, durative process of traversing multiple cities that necessarily culminates in a defined terminal point (completion of going through all cities), with an inherent process phase leading toward that completion.
AktAch_explanation: Have gone through describes a process of traversing multiple cities over time rather than a punctual change-of-state event at a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities' describes a bounded, completive action with an inherent endpoint (finishing all cities), not a dynamic durative situation without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have gone through entails a resulting change of state (completion of traversing cities), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through' describes a dynamic, progressive action with an inherent endpoint (completing passage through all cities), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'before you have gone through all the cities of Israel' describes a prospective, conditional future event, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have gone through' describes a prospective event that is expected to occur (before the Son of man comes), not an event construed as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have gone through' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'before' is a conjunction introducing a temporal clause, not an adverbial modifier of the target verb phrase.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities of Israel' presents the event of going through cities as part of a potential life history—answering whether this occurrence will happen at least once—without asserting that a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'have gone through' indicates a change of state (from not having gone through cities to having gone through them) with the resulting state of completion holding at the reference time of the Son of man's arrival.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through' expresses a completed action (perfective aspect) that will be finished before a future event, not a state or activity holding continuously from past to present reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have gone through' presents the completion of going through all cities as a bounded whole event, with the transition itself construed as a single completed action prior to the Son of man's arrival.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have gone through all the cities of Israel' is embedded in a conditional/temporal clause projecting a hypothetical future scenario ('before you have gone through...the Son of man will come'), construing the situation as non-actual and anticipated rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have gone through' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have gone through' is active voice with 'you' as the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of going through cities, not a passive construction where the patient functions as subject.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'has been' is a copular auxiliary expressing a stative relation (kingdom of heaven = man's goal) rather than a two-participant transitive event directed from agent toward affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'has been' is an auxiliary verb form licensing a two-participant predicate ('man's goal') where the subject 'kingdom of heaven' functions as an external argument, not a sole internal argument theme/patient.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been' is an auxiliary verb in a passive construction ('has been man's goal'), which licenses an internal argument (the subject 'kingdom of heaven'), violating the requirement for zero internal arguments in ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a stative condition (the kingdom as a goal) that persists from past to present without inherent culmination or terminal point, failing to meet the requirement of a dynamic event with defined completion.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has been' expresses a state of continuous duration from past to present rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a stative condition (the kingdom being a goal) with a defined result state achieved from a past point to present, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a stative condition (the kingdom being man's goal) with a resulting state that persists from past to present, not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has been' describes the kingdom of heaven as man's goal in a state that holds continuously from the past until now without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, matching the AktState definition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'has been' describes a factual state that actually occurred (the kingdom of heaven serving as man's goal from John the Baptist until now), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has been' presents the kingdom of heaven as man's goal as a realized and ongoing state (from past to present), not as absent or non-occurring.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains a temporal frame ('From the days of John the Baptist until now') that establishes a time interval, but this is a prepositional phrase indicating duration from past to present, not an overt adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval as required by DtDTAM.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'has been' asserts that the kingdom of heaven currently holds the state of being 'man's goal' from past to present, rather than presenting the event as a completed occurrence in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has been' indicates a change of state from the kingdom of heaven not being man's goal to it becoming man's goal (from the days of John the Baptist), with this resulting state holding at the reference time (now).
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has been' expresses a state (the kingdom of heaven being man's goal) that holds continuously from a past starting point ('from the days of John the Baptist') up to the reference time ('until now'), directly instantiating the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has been' presents an ongoing state from past to present rather than a bounded whole or completed transition, failing to meet the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has been' asserts the kingdom of heaven as a realized fact (man's actual goal from John the Baptist until now), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has been' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a progressive perfect form (present perfect progressive) rather than the simple present perfect, as 'has been' + adjective/noun functions as a periphrastic construction distinct from canonical 'have/has' + past participle.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been' is an auxiliary construction marking a perfect aspect, not passive voice; the semantic patient 'kingdom of heaven' functions as the grammatical subject, but there is no overt passive morphology (no past participle following 'has been'), and the predicate 'man's goal' is adjectival rather than a passive verb form.

FullText: » From the days of John the Baptist until now , the kingdom of heaven has been man’s goal , and men of violence have attempted to take it by force .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an external argument ('men of violence') as the agent performing the action, and the internal argument is the infinitival complement 'to take it', making this a two-participant event structure that does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'attempted' licenses an internal argument (the infinitival complement 'to take it by force'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have attempted' denotes an action directed toward a goal but does not inherently culminate in a defined terminal point or result state; attempting does not necessarily entail completion or achievement of the goal.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have attempted' describes a repeated or habitual action over an extended period ('from the days of John the Baptist until now') rather than a punctual change-of-state event at a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have attempted' describes a bounded action with an inherent endpoint (the attempt itself) and an implied result state (success or failure in taking the kingdom), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have attempted' denotes an action with a resulting change of state (taking the kingdom by force), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have attempted' describes a dynamic action with inherent progression toward a goal (taking the kingdom by force), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have attempted' describes an actual historical event that did occur (men of violence actually attempted to take the kingdom by force), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have attempted' presents an event that is realized and occurring in reality (men of violence did attempt to take the kingdom by force), not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'until now' modifies the preceding clause, not the target clause.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have attempted' presents the event of attempting to take the kingdom by force as part of a historical life narrative without asserting that a resulting state currently holds, directly matching the DtExp feature definition.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'have attempted' describes a repeated or habitual action (men trying to take the kingdom by force) rather than a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect, so it does not instantiate DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have attempted' expresses a completed action (men of violence made attempts) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have attempted' presents the action of attempting as a bounded, completed event without exposing its internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have attempted' is asserted as a realized fact about men of violence in actual history, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have attempted' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have attempted' is active voice with 'men of violence' as the grammatical subject performing the action, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive voice construction 'had been done' lacks an explicit external argument (agent) and does not encode a directed transitive event from an agent toward a patient, as the agent is suppressed and the construction emphasizes the affected internal argument rather than a two-participant transitive relation.
ASUncc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' encodes the event of doing with only one participant (the implicit agent) functioning as an internal argument (theme/patient of the passive), and the verb does not license an external argument in the surface structure.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction with 'done' as the lexical verb, which encodes a two-participant event (agent performing an action on a patient), not a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction describing a completed state rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction describing a completed state rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it represents the result of an action rather than the boundary point of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a perfective past passive construction describing a completed action with a result state, not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed action with a resulting state (mighty works accomplished), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the performance of mighty works), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon' presents a counterfactual condition: the works were not actually done in those cities, but the speaker presents this as a hypothetical contrary-to-fact scenario to support the consequent that those cities would have repented.
ClNegPol_explanation: The counterfactual conditional construction 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon' presents the event of mighty works occurring in those cities as not actually realized in reality, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had been done' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'long ago' modifies the consequent clause, not the target clause.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) expressing a hypothetical scenario, not a past event in the subject's life history, so it does not instantiate DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive in a counterfactual conditional clause expressing an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been done' expresses a counterfactual past event in a conditional clause, not a continuous state from a past starting point to reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The form 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction that presents the situation as anterior to another past event (the hypothetical repentance), making internal temporal development accessible through the perfect aspect rather than bounding the situation as a single completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been done' appears in a conditional protasis ('If the mighty works had been done...') expressing a counterfactual/hypothetical situation that did not occur in actuality, thus instantiating the TLMdIrr feature of non-actual construal.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, combining 'had' (past auxiliary) with the perfect participle 'been done'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates VcPass: the semantic patient (the mighty works) functions as the grammatical subject, the agent is omitted, and the construction includes overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'had been' + past participle 'done').

FullText: » Woe to you , Chorazin ! Woe to you , Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon that were done in you , they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' is intransitive, encoding a single-participant event of internal psychological change without an external argument directing action toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repented' licenses an external argument (the subject 'they'), making it a two-participant event where the subject is an agent/experiencer rather than a theme/patient, thus failing the ASUncc criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument 'they' functions as the external argument (agent/initiator performing the action of repenting), with no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'would have repented' describes a dynamic, durative process of changing one's mind and behavior that necessarily culminates in a defined result state (the completion of repentance), with an inherent process phase leading toward that completion.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a hypothetical state change that is conceived as an unfolding process of inner transformation rather than a punctual threshold-crossing event, lacking the instantaneous boundary-point semantics required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a punctual, completed action with a defined endpoint (the state of having repented), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have repented' denotes a change of state (from not repenting to repenting) with a resulting condition, not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a dynamic change of state (from not repenting to repenting) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'they would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different conditions (if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon), indicating a situation contrary to actual fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The conditional construction 'If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented' presents repentance as a counterfactual event that did not occur in reality, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is explicitly anchored to a specific past time interval by the overt adverbial 'long ago', which directly modifies the event and specifies when the repentance would have occurred.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different circumstances, not an event presented as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened in an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not a change of state that has actually occurred or holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have repented' expresses a counterfactual past event that would have occurred at a specific point in time ('long ago'), not a continuous state or activity extending from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The form 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical situation, not a bounded completed event, thus it does not instantiate perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The construction 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on the unfulfilled condition 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon,' thus instantiating the TLMdIrr feature of non-actual, projected meaning.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have repented' encodes both conditional mood (would) and perfect aspect (have + past participle), representing a modal perfect form that deviates from the simple present perfect canonical form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice with 'they' as the grammatical subject performing the action of repenting; it lacks the passive voice morphology (be + past participle) and does not express a semantic patient as the grammatical subject.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'do' does not encode a two-participant event directed from an external agent toward an affected internal argument, as the agent is demoted/suppressed and the theme is promoted to subject position.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where 'done' encodes a one-participant event with the implicit theme/patient as the sole argument, and no external argument is licensed in the surface structure.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'do' licenses an internal argument (the implied object of the action) as the subject, violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg feature instantiation.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates AktAcc as it describes a dynamic event (performing mighty works) with a defined terminal point (completion of the works) that would have necessarily culminated in a result state (Sodom's preservation).
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive construction describing a hypothetical completed state rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed, perfective action with a defined result state (the performance of mighty works), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' expresses a hypothetical past perfect passive construction that entails a resulting change of state (the works would have produced effects in Sodom), not a punctual non-resultative event.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed action with a clear endpoint (the performance of mighty works), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'If the mighty works had been done in Sodom' presents a counterfactual condition: the works were not actually done in Sodom (they were done in Capernaum instead), yet the speaker presents this as a hypothetical possibility with contrary-to-fact implications for what would have resulted.
ClNegPol_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'If the mighty works had been done in Sodom' presents the event of mighty works occurring in Sodom as not actually realized, construing it as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had been done' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'in Sodom' is locative, not temporal.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a counterfactual conditional (contrary-to-fact) that does not assert the event occurred in the subject's life history, but rather presents a hypothetical scenario that did not happen.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive in a counterfactual conditional clause expressing an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been done' expresses a counterfactual past condition in a conditional statement, not a continuous state from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive form that presents a hypothetical, unrealized condition rather than a bounded completed event; it does not instantiate perfective aspect as it expresses counterfactual possibility within a conditional structure, not a situation construed as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been done' appears in a conditional clause ('If the mighty works had been done in Sodom') that expresses a counterfactual/hypothetical situation contrary to fact, not asserted as realized.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'been' + past participle 'done'), the semantic patient ('mighty works') functions as the grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » Will you , Capernaum , be exalted into heaven ? You will go down into the grave . If the mighty works had been done in Sodom , which were done in you , it would have remained until this day .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'remained' encodes a stative event of persistence/continuation with a single participant (Sodom), lacking the two-participant transitive structure with external agent and affected internal argument required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' encodes a two-participant event (Sodom as external argument/subject and the state of standing as internal argument), licensing an external argument, thus does not instantiate ASUncc.

AktAch_explanation: Would have remained expresses a state of continuation or persistence, not a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have remained' expresses a stative condition with an inherent endpoint (until this day), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Would have remained is a stative verb expressing a resulting state of persistence over time, not a dynamic punctual event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have remained' describes a state (Sodom's standing/existence) that would hold continuously over time without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, matching the AktState feature definition.

ClNegPol_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have remained' presents a hypothetical unrealized state (Sodom not being destroyed) that did not occur in reality, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would have remained' lacks an overt adverbial explicitly anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'until this day' modifies the duration of the state, not the time of the event itself.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have remained' expresses a counterfactual conditional state that did not occur, indicating no actual change of state or resulting state currently in effect, thus failing to instantiate DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have remained' expresses a counterfactual state that would have held continuously from a past point (if the mighty works had been done in Sodom) until the reference time (this day), instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would have remained' presents a hypothetical state of continuation rather than a bounded completed event; it construes the situation as ongoing until a specified point, not as a perfective whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have remained' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical situation that did not occur in reality, as indicated by the preceding 'If the mighty works had been done in Sodom' clause.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have remained' is an active voice construction where the subject 'it' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient; there is no overt passive morphology (no 'be' + past participle form) and no by-phrase indicating an external agent.

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (You/God) as agent and an internal argument (these things) as patient, with the action directed from agent toward patient and affecting it.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' licenses an external argument (You) and an internal argument (these things), encoding a two-participant event where the subject is the agent performing the hiding action, not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have hidden' licenses an internal argument ('these things'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have hidden' instantiates AktAcc as it describes a dynamic action (hiding) that unfolds over time with a defined terminal point (the state of things being hidden from the wise and understanding).
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have hidden' describes a completed state of concealment rather than a punctual change-of-state event at a threshold boundary; it presents the result of hiding as a persistent condition, not the instantaneous crossing into hiddenness.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have hidden' describes a completed action with a result state (things are now hidden), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have hidden describes a completed action with a resulting change of state (things are now hidden from the wise), not a punctual event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have hidden' describes a completed action with a resultant state rather than a situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change, as it involves the dynamic event of concealing things from certain people.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding' describes an actual event that has occurred in the world of the text, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have hidden' describes an event that has occurred and is realized in reality (you have hidden these things), not an absent or non-occurring state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have hidden' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'Then' modifies the prayer event, not the hiding event itself.

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have hidden' expresses a state (concealment of things) that began in the past and continues to hold relevance at the reference time of the prayer, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have hidden' presents the action of hiding as a completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing it as a bounded whole without internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have hidden' is asserted as a realized fact within Jesus's prayer addressing God's actual actions, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have hidden' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have hidden' instantiates VcPass because the semantic patient 'these things' functions as the grammatical subject, the agent (You/God) is expressed as the subject of the clause, and the construction includes overt passive morphology ('have hidden' as passive voice form).

FullText: Then Jesus prayed : » I thank you , O Father , Lord of heaven and earth . You have hidden these things from the wise and understanding , and have revealed them to children .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'revealed' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (You/God as agent) and an internal argument (them/things as patient), where the action is directed from the agent toward the patient and affects it by making it known.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'revealed' is transitive and licenses an external argument (You), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'revealed' licenses an internal argument ('them'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have revealed' denotes a dynamic action of disclosing or making known that unfolds through a process and culminates in a defined result state (things becoming known to children), instantiating AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have revealed' instantiates AktAch as it denotes a punctual change-of-state event where something hidden crosses the threshold into being made known, representing the boundary point of transition rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: Have revealed entails a resulting change of state (things becoming known to children), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have revealed' expresses an action that began in the past and extends to the reference time (the moment of speaking), indicating a state of continuous relevance from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have revealed' presents the act of revealing as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition itself as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have revealed' is asserted as a realized fact within Jesus's prayer of thanksgiving, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or non-actual.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . Only the Father knows the Son , and only the Son knows the Father . The Son tells others about the Father so that they can know him too .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'delivered' encodes a two-participant event with an external agent (implied subject, the Father) and an internal patient (things), where the action is directed from agent toward patient and affects it by transferring possession.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' is passive voice with 'all things' as the internal argument, but it licenses an external argument (the agent 'from my Father'), violating the CRI requirement that the verb not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' is a passive construction with an internal argument ('All things' as the patient/theme), not a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect passive 'have been delivered' describes a dynamic process of transfer that culminates in a defined result state (completion of delivery), meeting the AktAcc criterion of a durative event with inherent process leading to a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates AktAch as it describes a punctual change-of-state event where things transition from not-delivered to delivered status, representing the boundary point of that transfer rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' is a perfective passive construction indicating a completed action with a result state (things are now in a delivered condition), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' describes a resultant state (things now being in a delivered condition) rather than a punctual dynamic event, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel which requires a bounded occurrence without consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' describes a completed action with a resultant state, involving dynamic change and an inherent endpoint, which contradicts the stative, unchanging nature required by AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All things have been delivered to me from my Father' describes an actual state of affairs presented as factual, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' presents a realized, completed event (perfect aspect) where things were actually delivered to the speaker, not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' presents the delivery event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' indicates a completed action (delivery of things) with the resulting state (things now in the possession of the speaker) holding at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' expresses a state resulting from a past action that extends to the reference time, indicating that the delivery is complete and relevant at the moment of speaking, which instantiates the DtUniv feature of continuous relevance from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' presents the delivery as a completed, bounded event with resultant state, construing the transition to delivery as a single whole without internal temporal development, which instantiates TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a progressive perfect form (present perfect passive with 'been') rather than the simple present perfect, thus specifying an aspectual feature beyond the canonical 'have/has' + past participle structure.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates VcPass: the semantic patient 'All things' functions as grammatical subject, the agent is omitted, and overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'have been' + past participle 'delivered') is present.

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'know' in 'had known' is a stative verb encoding a mental state rather than a two-participant event directed from an external argument toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'known' encodes a two-participant event (knower and known object) where 'you' is an external argument (experiencer/agent), violating the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement of ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'known' licenses an internal argument (the object clause 'what this means'), so it does not encode a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Had known is a past perfect form expressing a stative condition (knowledge possession) rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: Had known is a past perfect form expressing a state of knowledge rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: Had known is a past perfect form expressing a completed mental state with a defined endpoint (the moment of knowing), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Had known is a stative past perfect expressing a resultant state of knowledge rather than a dynamic punctual event, and it entails a resulting change of state (from not knowing to knowing), thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had known' describes a completed cognitive state with a definite endpoint (the moment of knowing), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'If you had known' presents a counterfactual condition—the speaker asserts that the addressee did not actually know this meaning, yet describes what would have followed if they had, making it contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'had known' presents the event of knowing as not actually occurring in reality, construing it as absent rather than realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure alone does not constitute such an anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) expressing an unrealized hypothetical event, not an event presented as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had known' is a counterfactual past perfect in a conditional clause expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had known' is a past perfect counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical state at a single past point, not a continuous state from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The form 'had known' is a past perfect counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not a bounded completed event; it presents the internal temporal development of knowing as accessible through the conditional structure rather than as a single bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had known' appears in a conditional protasis ('If you had known...') that construes a counterfactual, hypothetical situation contrary to fact, not asserted as realized in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had known' is a past perfect form (pluperfect), which encodes a tense specification (past) other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is an active voice construction where the subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of knowing, not the patient; there is no overt passive morphology or by-phrase present.

FullText: » If you had known what this means , ‘ I desire mercy and not sacrifice , ’ ’ you would not have condemned the guiltless .

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' entails a resulting change of state (the guiltless would remain uncondemned), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' describes a dynamic action with an inherent endpoint (the act of condemning), not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different conditions (if you had known), indicating an event understood not to have occurred in the actual world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' explicitly negates the event of condemning through the negative particle 'not', presenting the act of condemnation as unrealized/absent from the hypothetical reality.

DtExp_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would not have happened under hypothetical circumstances, not a life history event that has occurred or been experienced.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized past state, not a change of state that holds at the reference time, thus it does not instantiate DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' expresses a counterfactual conditional outcome in a specific past situation, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical situation, which presents the situation as open or negated rather than as a bounded completed whole, thus failing to instantiate the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The construction 'would not have condemned' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on an unfulfilled condition ('If you had known'), thus instantiating the TLMdIrr feature of non-actual/projected meaning.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a modal specification (would) combined with perfect aspect (have + past participle), which is a modal perfect form distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have condemned' is active voice with 'you' as the grammatical subject performing the action of condemning; the patient 'the guiltless' is the direct object, not the subject, and there is no passive morphological marking.

FullText: » Look ! My servant whom I have chosen . I am well pleased with him . I will put my Spirit upon him and he will declare judgment to the nations .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'chosen' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the speaker/agent) and an internal argument (the servant/patient), where the action is directed from the agent toward the patient and affects the patient's status.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have chosen' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (implicit agent 'I') and an internal argument ('whom'), so it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with only an internal argument.

AktSemel_explanation: Have chosen entails a resulting change of state (the servant is now in a chosen condition) and is not punctual/instantaneous, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have chosen' is a perfective aspect expressing a completed action with a definite endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have chosen' presents a completed action as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.

DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have chosen' presents the choosing as a completed event in the subject's history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds, directly matching the DtExp feature definition.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have chosen' indicates a completed action (choosing the servant) whose resulting state (the servant being chosen) holds at the reference time, encoding a transition from not-chosen to chosen state.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have chosen' expresses a past action (the choosing) with present relevance, indicating a state that holds from the past choosing point to the reference time of speaking.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have chosen' presents the choice as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present moment, construing the selection as a single accomplished whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have chosen' is presented as a completed past action asserted as an actual fact within the speaker's declaration, not as a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.

AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has come' entails a resulting change of state (the kingdom of God is now present), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' describes a dynamic change with an inherent endpoint (the arrival of the kingdom), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of God has come to you' presents a factual assertion about a real event that has occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has come' presents the kingdom of God as having realized/occurred (positive polarity), not as absent from reality, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'then' is a conditional conjunction, not a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' asserts that the kingdom of God has arrived and currently holds a resulting state of presence, rather than merely recording whether the event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' indicates a change of state (the kingdom of God transitioning from not-present to present) with the resulting state holding at the reference time, matching the DtRes feature definition.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' expresses that the kingdom of God's arrival (a state/event) holds from a past point up to the reference time, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' presents the arrival of God's kingdom as a bounded, completed transition event without exposing its internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has come' asserts the kingdom of God as a realized fact in the actual world, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'come') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice with the semantic agent ('kingdom of God') as grammatical subject, lacking passive morphology and a by-phrase, thus does not instantiate VcPass.

FullText: » Have you understood all these things ? « They said to him : » Yes . «

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'understood' is a two-participant event requiring an external argument (the experiencer 'you') and an internal argument (the theme 'all these things'), so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'understood' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('all these things'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Have understood is a perfect aspect form denoting a completed mental state rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' describes a state resulting from a prior action rather than a punctual change-of-state event; understanding is a cognitive process without a definable threshold moment, not a dynamic transition crossing a boundary.

AktSemel_explanation: Have understood is a perfective achievement verb that entails a resulting change of state (transition from not understanding to understanding), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: Have understood is a perfective aspect marking completion of a dynamic change (from not understanding to understanding), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without inherent progression.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have understood' is used in a yes/no question asking whether understanding occurred, not negating or presenting the understanding as absent from reality; the affirmative response 'Yes' confirms the understanding as realized rather than negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether understanding has occurred at least once without asserting current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' encodes a transition from a state of not understanding to a state of understanding, with the resulting state of comprehension holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have understood' is a present perfect used in a yes/no question about a completed action, not expressing a state or activity that holds continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have understood' presents the mental state of understanding as a bounded, completed event without exposing its internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have understood' is used in a direct question about a realized state of comprehension, asserting actual understanding as fact rather than projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have understood' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt encoding of tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have understood' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent performing the understanding, not the patient; there is no overt passive morphology or by-phrase agent expression.

FullText: And he said to them : » Every scribe journalist writer who has been made a disciple to the kingdom of heaven is like a man that is a householder , who brings out of his treasure things new and old . «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'has been made a disciple' instantiates ASTrns because the underlying lexical verb 'make' encodes a two-participant event with an external agent (implied) directing an action toward an internal patient (the scribe/journalist/writer) that results in a change of state (becoming a disciple).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' in the passive construction 'has been made a disciple' licenses an external argument (the agent performing the making, implied but present in the passive voice structure), so it does not meet the criterion of encoding a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been made' is a passive construction with an internal argument ('a disciple'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.

AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has been made a disciple' describes a completed state resulting from a past action, not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been made a disciple' entails a resulting change of state (becoming a disciple), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has been made' describes a completed action with a resultant state (becoming a disciple), which involves dynamic change and has an inherent endpoint, thus does not instantiate AktState.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has been made' presents a realized state (someone who has become a disciple), not an absent or non-occurring event, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has been made' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'has been made a disciple' presents the event of becoming a disciple as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has been made' encodes a change of state from not being a disciple to becoming a disciple, with the resulting state (being a disciple) holding at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has been made' expresses a completed action (passive perfect) resulting in a state, not a continuous activity from past to present as required by DtUniv.

FullText: For John had said to him : » It is not lawful for you to have her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'said' encodes a two-participant event with John as external argument (agent/initiator) and 'him' as internal argument (patient/theme), where the speech act is directed from John toward him and affects him by conveying information.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' encodes a two-participant event (sayer and addressee) with an external argument (John as agent), not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'said' licenses an internal argument (the reported speech clause 'It is not lawful for you to have her'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had said' denotes a completed speech act with no inherent process phase or durative unfolding toward a terminal point, thus does not instantiate AktAcc.

AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had said' describes a completed speech act with a resulting state (the utterance having been made and its content established), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had said' is a past perfect form that locates a prior speech event before another past event, rather than indicating a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.

FullText: » You say there is no need for him to give honor to his father . You have invalidated the word of God because of your tradition !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'invalidated' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (You) as agent and an internal argument (the word of God) as patient, where the action is directed from agent toward patient and affects it by rendering it null or void.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' is transitive and licenses an external argument (You), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have invalidated' is transitive with an internal argument ('the word of God'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires a one-participant event with no internal argument licensed.

AktAch_explanation: The perfective aspect of 'have invalidated' presents the action as a completed change-of-state event where God's word transitions from valid to invalid status, crossing a definable threshold at a punctual moment rather than depicting an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: Have invalidated describes a resultative action that entails a change of state (the word of God has been rendered invalid), not a punctual event without consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have invalidated' describes a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint (the act of invalidating), not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have invalidated the word of God' describes an actual event that has occurred in the real world according to the speaker's assertion, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have invalidated' presents a realized action (you have done this), not an absent or non-occurring event, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'have invalidated' is present perfect but lacks temporal anchoring adverbials like 'already', 'just', or specific time expressions.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have invalidated' presents the action as part of the addressee's life history, asserting that the event of invalidating God's word has occurred at least once without claiming a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have invalidated' indicates a completed action (invalidating God's word) whose resulting state (the word being invalidated) holds at the reference time, encoding a transition from a state where the word was valid to a current state where it is invalid.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have invalidated' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the DtUniv criterion of expressing continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have invalidated' presents the action of invalidating as a completed, bounded whole event with relevance to the present moment, consistent with perfective aspect construal.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have invalidated' is asserted as a realized fact about the addressee's actual actions, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have invalidated' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: In answer he replied : » Every plant my Father in heaven has not planted in the earth will be pulled out by the roots .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'planted' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (Father) as agent/initiator and an internal argument (plant) as patient/theme, with the event directed from agent toward and affecting the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'planted' is transitive and licenses an external argument (the Father as agent), with 'plant' encoding a two-participant event where the subject is the planter and the object is the theme, violating the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'planted' licenses an internal argument ('Every plant') as its direct object, violating the one-participant event requirement where only an external argument is permitted.
AktAcc_explanation: The negated perfect aspect 'has not planted' describes a state of non-action rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.

AktSemel_explanation: The negated perfect 'has not planted' describes a state of non-action with a resulting condition (plants not existing in the earth), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
