ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has not planted' presents the event of planting as not occurring—plants that the Father has not planted are construed as absent from the Father's action, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has not planted' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'has not planted' expresses a negated state of non-planting rather than presenting an event as part of a life history; it describes what the Father has not done, not what has occurred in the subject's experiential past.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'has not planted' describes a state of non-action (absence of planting) rather than a change of state or resulting state; it does not encode a transition from State A to State B with a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has not planted' expresses a negated perfect state (non-action up to reference time) rather than a continuous activity or habitual series holding from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'has not planted' presents a negative perfect state rather than a bounded completed event; it describes what has not occurred, not a transition construed as a single completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has not planted' appears in a conditional/universal statement about plants that will be pulled out, construing a hypothetical or non-actual situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has not planted' is a canonical present perfect form (negated) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not planted' is active voice with the agent (Father) as grammatical subject and the patient (plant) as object, not passive voice where the patient would be subject.

FullText: Jesus called his disciples and said : » I feel compassion for the people , because they have remained with me now three days and have nothing to eat . I do not want to send them away hungry , for they might faint on the way . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'remained' encodes a stative event of continuous presence without an external agent directing an action toward an affected patient, failing to instantiate the two-participant transitive event structure required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'remained' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the people) and licenses a locative complement (with me), not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'remained' licenses an internal argument ('with me'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: Have remained describes a stative condition of continuous presence without an inherent terminal point or defined result state, lacking the dynamic culmination required by AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: Have remained describes a continuous state of being present over three days rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: Have remained describes a stative result of a prior action (staying with Jesus), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement, as it expresses a completed state rather than an ongoing process.
AktSemel_explanation: Have remained is a stative verb expressing a resulting state of continuous presence over three days, not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have remained' describes a state of continuous presence over three days without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, matching the AktState definition of a situation that holds over time qualitatively identical throughout.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'they have remained with me now three days' describes an actual event that has occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have remained' describes an event that has occurred and is realized in reality (the people stayed with Jesus for three days), not an absent or non-occurring situation.

DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have remained' expresses a state that currently holds (they are still with him now) rather than merely asserting that an event occurred at least once in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: Have remained describes a continuous state (people staying with Jesus for three days) rather than a change of state or transition from State A to State B, so it does not instantiate DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have remained' expresses a state (remaining with Jesus) that began in the past (three days ago) and continues up to the reference time (now), matching the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have remained' presents an ongoing state from past to present rather than a bounded whole event, focusing on the continuous duration of their stay rather than a completed transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have remained with me now three days' is asserted as a realized fact within the narrative world, not as a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have remained' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have remained' is an active voice construction where 'they' (the disciples) is the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of remaining, not a patient; there is no by-phrase and no passive morphological marking.

FullText: When they came down from the mountain , Jesus commanded them not to tell anyone what they saw , until the Son of man has risen from the dead .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' licenses an external argument (the Son of man as agent/experiencer), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.

AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has risen from the dead' describes a dynamic, durative process of resurrection that unfolds over time and culminates in a defined terminal point (the completed state of being risen), meeting the AktAcc feature criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has risen from the dead' represents a punctual change-of-state event marking the boundary transition from death to life, conceived as a threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' describes a completed change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to risen), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without an inherent result.

AktState_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' describes a dynamic change with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to risen state), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen from the dead' presents an event as occurring or holding (a positive assertion of resurrection), not as absent from reality, thus does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has risen from the dead' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'until' is a conjunction, not an adverbial anchor for the target clause itself.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram 'has risen from the dead' presents a future event contingent on a condition ('until'), not a past event in the subject's life history, so it does not instantiate DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has risen from the dead' encodes a change of state from dead to alive, with the resulting state (being alive) presented as holding at the reference time, matching the DtRes feature definition.
DtUniv_explanation: The target gram 'has risen from the dead' expresses a future perfective event (completion of resurrection) rather than a state holding continuously from past to reference time, as the intuition requires.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has risen from the dead' presents the transition of resurrection as a single bounded, completed event without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' is embedded in a temporal clause ('until the Son of man has risen from the dead') that projects a future, hypothetical event as a condition for when the disciples may speak, rather than asserting it as an already-realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen from the dead' is active voice with 'Son of man' as the grammatical subject performing the action, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: » I tell you that Elijah has come . They did not know him and treated him badly as they pleased . They will do the same with the Son of man . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' encodes a one-participant event of motion/arrival with only an internal argument (Elijah), lacking the two-participant agent-patient structure required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' in 'has come' is an intransitive verb that licenses an external argument (Elijah as the subject/agent), not an internal argument, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' in 'has come' encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument 'Elijah' functions as the external argument (agent/initiator), and no internal argument is licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' describes a completed state rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a terminal point; it presents arrival as a resultant state, not an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has come' expresses a perfective aspect indicating a completed action with a resultant state (arrival), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' describes a completed action with a resulting state (Elijah's arrival and presence), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' describes a completed dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (arrival), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' describes a factual assertion about an event that is presented as having occurred in reality, not as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has come' presents Elijah's arrival as a realized event that occurred in reality, not as absent or non-occurring, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Elijah has come' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has come' presents Elijah's arrival as an event that has occurred at least once in the subject's life history without asserting a current resulting state, directly instantiating the DtExp feature.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has come' encodes a transition from a state where Elijah had not arrived to a state where Elijah has arrived, with this resulting state holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has come' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the CRI requirement of holding 'continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' presents the arrival of Elijah as a bounded, completed event viewed as a whole without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has come' is asserted as a realized fact within the speaker's statement, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'come') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice with 'Elijah' as the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of coming, not a patient receiving the action.

FullText: » What do you think ? A man has a hundred sheep , and one of them has gone wandering away . Will he leave the ninety-nine and go in search of the wandering one ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'go wandering' encodes a one-participant event (the sheep as agent/theme) with no external argument directing action toward an internal argument; the sheep's wandering is self-directed motion, not a transitive event affecting a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gone wandering' encodes a two-participant event where 'one' (the sheep) functions as an external argument (agent/experiencer) performing the action of wandering, not as an internal argument, thus failing the ASUncc criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'gone wandering' takes an internal argument (the sheep as the entity that has wandered away), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has gone wandering' describes a dynamic event (the sheep's departure) that unfolded over time and culminated in a defined result state (the sheep being away/lost), with an inherent process phase leading to completion.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has gone wandering' describes a completed action with a resultant state (the sheep is now away/lost), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has gone wandering' entails a resulting change of state (the sheep is now away from the flock), which violates the AktSemel requirement that there be no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' describes a dynamic change (a sheep departing from the flock) with an inherent endpoint (the completion of the wandering action), which contradicts the stative nature required by AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has gone wandering' describes a hypothetical situation presented as a possible condition ('one of them has gone wandering away'), not a counterfactual state understood as contrary to actual fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' describes an event that has occurred and is realized in the scenario (one sheep has actually wandered away), not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'has gone wandering' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' presents the event as part of the sheep's life history, answering whether the wandering has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' encodes a transition from a state where the sheep was present to a state where it is wandering away, with this resulting state holding at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The gram 'has gone wandering' expresses a completed action with present relevance (one sheep departed), not a continuous state from past to present as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has gone wandering' presents the action of going astray as a bounded, completed event without internal temporal development, treating the transition itself as a single whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' is asserted as a realized fact within the hypothetical scenario (the sheep has actually wandered away), not as a projected or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' is a canonical present perfect form (has + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has gone wandering' is active voice with the semantic agent (the sheep) as grammatical subject, lacking the passive voice morphological marking and patient-as-subject structure required for VcPass.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction where the lexical verb 'fill' does not encode an external argument as the grammatical subject directing action toward an internal argument; instead, the subject 'he' is the patient/theme receiving the action, violating the ASTrns requirement for a two-participant transitive event with agent-to-patient directionality.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a conditional modal construction, not a lexical verb encoding a one-participant event; the passive voice structure 'been filled' licenses an implicit external argument (agent), violating the ASUncc criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a passive construction where the sole argument (he) functions as an internal argument (patient/theme) receiving the action, not as an external argument/agent, violating the ASUnrg requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical state rather than a dynamic event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' describes a resultant state of being filled with joy rather than a dynamic durative process unfolding over time, lacking the sustained engagement and processual nature required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' expresses a hypothetical state of being filled with joy, which entails a resulting change of state (from not-filled to filled), thus it does not instantiate AktSemel, which requires no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' describes a stative condition of being in a state of joy that holds over time without dynamic change or progression, matching the AktState feature definition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a contrary-to-fact situation: it describes what would have happened if he had stayed with the ninety-nine, which did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' presents a counterfactual conditional state that did not occur, construing the situation as absent from reality—the shepherd would have experienced less joy if he had stayed with the ninety-nine, but he did not stay, so this state is not realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional without an overt adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the temporal reference is implicit in the conditional structure, not marked by an overt temporal adverb.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not a past event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' expresses a counterfactual conditional state in the past, not a continuous state from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state rather than a bounded completed event, lacking the perfective aspect's characteristic presentation of a situation as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact, instantiating the TLMdIrr feature.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have' instantiates TLTS because it encodes modal specification (conditional 'would') combined with perfect aspect, which is a non-canonical perfect form distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have (been filled with joy)' is a conditional passive construction, but the actual instantiation in the text is 'would have if he had stayed', which is a conditional clause without the passive voice morphology; the passive 'be filled with joy' appears in the main clause, not in the target conditional structure.

FullText: » When he finds it , I tell you he will be filled with more joy than he would have if he had stayed with the ninety-nine .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'stayed' encodes a two-participant event requiring an external argument (the subject 'he') and licenses an internal locative argument ('with the ninety-nine'), so it does not instantiate ASUncc.

AktAct_explanation: Had stayed is a perfective past conditional expressing a completed state with an inherent endpoint (remaining with the ninety-nine), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Had stayed is a stative verb phrase describing a continuous state of remaining in a location, not a dynamic punctual event without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had stayed' describes a state of remaining in a location that holds over time without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, fitting the AktState feature definition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'if he had stayed with the ninety-nine' presents a counterfactual condition contrary to the actual situation described in the narrative, using the past perfect construction to indicate an event that did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had stayed' describes a hypothetical condition in a counterfactual construction ('if he had stayed'), presenting a possible state rather than negating or construing an event as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he had stayed with the ninety-nine' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('if he had stayed') expressing a hypothetical alternative scenario, not presenting an event as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had stayed' is a counterfactual conditional in a hypothetical scenario (introduced by 'if'), not indicating an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had stayed' is a counterfactual conditional form expressing a hypothetical past state that did not occur, not a continuous state from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had stayed' is a past perfect form in a counterfactual conditional clause expressing an unrealized hypothetical situation, not a bounded completed event presented as a whole, thus it does not instantiate perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had stayed' appears in a counterfactual conditional clause ('if he had stayed') expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to what actually occurred.

FullText: » If your brother does wrong to you , go to him in private . Clearly explain his error between you and him . If he listens to you , you have gained your brother back .

DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have gained back' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the CRI requirement of continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.

FullText: » When the other servants saw what happened , they were very sad . They told their lord what had been done .

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is past perfect passive describing a completed action prior to the reference time (when they told their lord), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been done' expresses a completed past action (what happened to the lord's servants) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point up to the reference time.

FullText: » So they are no longer two , but one flesh . What God has joined together , let no man pull apart separate ! «

AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has joined together' describes a completed action with a resulting state (union of two into one flesh), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has joined together' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv which requires continuous holding from a past starting point.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has joined together' presents the joining as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present state (one flesh), construing the transition itself as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has joined together' is asserted as a realized fact (God's actual action of joining), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has joined together' is a canonical present perfect form (has + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: He said : » This does not apply to everyone , only to those God has given it to .

DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has given' expresses a completed past action (perfect aspect) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.

FullText: » For there are men who , from birth , were without sex . There are some who were made that way by men . There are others who have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven . He who is able to accept this let him accept it . «

DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'have made themselves so for the kingdom of heaven' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'have made themselves' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have made themselves' encodes a transition from a state of having sex to a state of being without sex, with the resulting state (being without sex) currently in effect at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have made themselves' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to reference time, lacking the durative aspect required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have made themselves' presents the self-castration as a completed, bounded whole event with relevance to the present state, instantiating perfective aspect.

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have done' expresses a completed action (keeping all commandments) that holds relevance from a past starting point up to the reference time of speaking, instantiating DtUniv.

FullText: Jesus replied : » Truly I say to you , when all things are made new , and the Son of man is seated in his glory , you who have come after me will be seated on twelve seats , judging the twelve tribes of Israel .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument 'after me' (a PP complement indicating direction/trajectory), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.

DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have come' expresses a completed action (those who followed Jesus) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from past to reference time.

FullText: » Everyone who has given up houses , or brothers , or sisters , or father , or mother , or child , or land , for my name , will receive many times as much , and have everlasting life .

FullText: » Concerning the resurrection of the dead , have you read that which was spoken to you by God ?

AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have read' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing, lacking the dynamic transition quality required for AktAch.

DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have read' presents the event of reading as part of the addressee's life history, asking whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'have read' encodes a change of state from not-having-read to having-read, with the resulting state (possession of the reading experience) holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have read' is a present perfect form expressing a past action (reading) with relevance to the present reference time, which aligns with the intuition of a state holding from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have read' presents the reading event as a completed, bounded whole without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have you read' is a direct question asking about a past action (reading), asserted as a real inquiry about actual experience, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or non-actual.

FullText: The Pharisees assembled together when they heard that he had put the Sadducees to silence .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' licenses an external argument (the Pharisees as agent) and an internal argument (the Sadducees as patient), making it a two-participant event that does not instantiate ASUncc.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' entails a resulting change of state (the Sadducees becoming silenced), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' describes a completed action with a clear endpoint (silencing the Sadducees), involving dynamic change, which contradicts the stative nature required for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause describes a factual event that occurred in the actual world (he did put the Sadducees to silence), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had put to silence' describes an event that occurred and was realized in reality (he successfully silenced the Sadducees), not an absent or non-occurring state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'when' is a temporal conjunction introducing a subordinate clause, not an adverbial modifier of the target verb phrase.
DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had put to silence' presents the silencing of the Sadducees as a completed event in the subject's life history, answering whether the event occurred without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' encodes a change of state from the Sadducees being able to speak to them being silenced, with the resulting state (silence) holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' is a past perfect form expressing a completed action prior to another past event (the Pharisees assembling), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had put to silence' presents the action of silencing the Sadducees as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (past perfect tense describing a completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'had put to silence' is active voice with 'he' as the grammatical subject performing the action and 'the Sadducees' as the semantic patient in object position, lacking the passive voice morphology and subject-patient alignment required for VcPass.

FullText: » Woe to you , scribes and Pharisees , hypocrites ! You tithe mint and dill and cumin , and have left undone the weightier matters of the law : justice , mercy and faith . You should do both and leave nothing undone .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' licenses an external argument (You) and encodes a two-participant event where 'the weightier matters' functions as an internal argument, failing to meet the one-participant, internal-argument-only requirement of ASUncc.

AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' describes a resulting state of neglect (matters remaining unattended) rather than a punctual, instantaneous event without consequent change, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the neglect of weightier matters), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.

ClNegPol_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' presents the weightier matters of the law as not occurring or not being realized, construing their absence from the Pharisees' actions as the actual state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have left undone' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' presents the action of neglecting weightier matters as part of the Pharisees' life history without asserting a current resulting state, answering whether the event has occurred.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' indicates a change of state from a previous state where these matters were potentially done to a current state where they remain undone, with this resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have left undone' expresses a completed action (neglecting weightier matters) rather than a state holding continuously from past to reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' presents the action of neglecting the weightier matters as a completed, bounded whole event without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' asserts a realized fact about what the scribes and Pharisees have actually done, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: » You claim that you would not have been involved with your fathers when they spilled the blood of the prophets .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'involved' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') and an internal argument ('with your fathers'), making it a two-participant event that does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' is a passive construction with an internal argument ('with your fathers'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event where the sole argument functions as an external agent with no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' expresses a counterfactual negative state without depicting a dynamic, durative event that necessarily culminates in a defined terminal point or result state.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' expresses a counterfactual state of non-participation rather than a dynamic change-of-state event at a threshold boundary.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' describes a state of non-participation or non-engagement rather than a dynamic, punctual event without resulting change of state, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target gram 'would not have been involved' describes a hypothetical counterfactual action with a clear endpoint (the act of involvement), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' uses the conditional 'would' with perfect aspect to express a counterfactual claim about a hypothetical past situation contrary to what actually occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' explicitly negates involvement through the negative particle 'not', presenting the event of being involved as absent from reality rather than realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional 'would' expresses hypothetical modality rather than temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram is a counterfactual conditional (would not have been involved) that expresses a hypothetical claim about non-involvement, not a presentation of an event as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical non-involvement in a past event, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target gram 'would not have been involved' expresses a counterfactual conditional about a past event, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' is a counterfactual conditional construction expressing a hypothetical unrealized situation, not a bounded perfective event presented as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact, instantiating the TLMdIrr feature.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a modal specification (would) combined with perfect aspect, which is a non-canonical perfect form distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' instantiates VcPass: the semantic patient (the subject 'you') functions as the grammatical subject, the agent is omitted, and the construction includes overt passive morphology ('been involved').

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'know' encodes a one-participant mental state (experiencer) rather than a two-participant event with an external agent directing an action toward an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' in 'had known' is a two-participant event requiring an external argument (the master as experiencer/knower) and an internal argument (the proposition about the thief's arrival), so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'know' in 'had known' licenses an internal argument (the clause 'what time the thief was coming'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had known' is a stative past perfect expressing a state of knowledge rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: Had known is a stative past perfect expressing a state of knowledge rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a threshold.
AktAct_explanation: Had known is a perfective past conditional expressing a completed mental state with a defined endpoint (acquiring knowledge), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Had known is a stative verb expressing a resulting state of knowledge rather than a dynamic punctual event, and it entails a change of state (from not knowing to knowing), which violates the AktSemel feature definition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had known' expresses a stative condition (possession of knowledge) that holds over time without dynamic change or progression, lacking an inherent endpoint, which instantiates the AktState feature.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had known what time the thief was coming' presents a counterfactual condition—the master did not actually know when the thief would come, yet the sentence presents this as a hypothetical condition contrary to fact that would have triggered different actions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized/non-occurring state: the master did not actually know what time the thief was coming, presenting the knowing event as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause containing 'had known' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'what time the thief was coming' is a complement clause, not a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional (if-clause) expressing an unrealized hypothetical event, not a past event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had known' is a counterfactual conditional in a past unreal condition, expressing a hypothetical state that did not occur, not an actual change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had known' is a counterfactual past perfect in a conditional clause expressing a hypothetical scenario, not a state holding continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had known' presents the state of knowing as a bounded, completed event prior to the conditional scenario, construing it as a perfective whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had known' appears in a counterfactual conditional structure ('if the master had known...he would have watched') that construes the situation as hypothetical and non-actual rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had known' is a past perfect form (pluperfect), which encodes a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is an active voice construction where the master (agent) is the grammatical subject performing the action of knowing, not a passive construction with the patient as subject.

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'watched' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the master/agent) directing attention toward and monitoring an internal argument (the house/theme), satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/watcher) and does not encode a one-participant event with the sole argument as internal argument, thus does not instantiate ASUncc.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have watched' denotes a durative activity (sustained vigilance over time) rather than a punctual, near-instantaneous event, and it entails a resulting change of state (prevention of vandalism), so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have watched' describes a dynamic action with inherent progression and a defined endpoint (the completion of watching), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized past event that would have occurred under different conditions (if the master had known the time), indicating a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The conditional structure 'if...had known...would have watched' presents the watching as a counterfactual event that did not occur in reality, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'what time' is a relative clause introducing a condition, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical event, not a past event presented as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have watched' expresses a counterfactual conditional past action (what would have occurred if a condition had been met), not a continuous state or activity holding from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' presents the hypothetical action as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on an unfulfilled condition ('if the master had known'), thus instantiating the TLMdIrr feature of non-actual construal.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have watched' encodes conditional mood (would) combined with perfect aspect, which is a tense/mood specification other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating TLTS.

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .

AktAcc_explanation: The target instantiates AktAcc because 'vandalized' describes a dynamic event (destruction of property) that unfolds through a process and culminates in a defined result state (the house being damaged), meeting the criteria of a durative event with inherent process leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: Vandalization represents a punctual change-of-state event where property transitions from intact to damaged at a definable threshold moment, instantiating AktAch.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have been vandalized' describes a resulting change of state (house remaining undamaged) rather than a punctual dynamic event, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target describes a dynamic change (vandalism occurring or not occurring) with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target clause presents a counterfactual condition: the house would not have been vandalized if the master had known when the thief was coming, but he did not know, so the vandalization is understood as having actually occurred contrary to this hypothetical scenario.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would not have been vandalized' presents the event of vandalization as not occurring in the counterfactual scenario, directly instantiating ClNegPol by construing the situation as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure indicates hypothetical pastness but lacks an explicit temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would not have happened under hypothetical circumstances, not a life history event that occurred at least once.
DtRes_explanation: The target gram is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened in an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not a change of state that has actually occurred with a resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target gram 'would not have been vandalized' expresses a counterfactual conditional outcome (what would have happened if the condition were met), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional perfect passive 'would not have been vandalized' presents the hypothetical situation of vandalism as a bounded, completed event without internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would not have been vandalized' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on an unmet condition ('if the master had known'), thus instantiating TLMdIrr.

FullText: » His lord said : ‘ Well done , good and faithful servant . You have been faithful over a few things , so I will put you in charge of many things . Enter into the joy of your lord . ’

AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have been faithful' describes a stative condition of loyalty maintained over time rather than a dynamic event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have been faithful' describes a state of continuous loyalty over time rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing, thus does not instantiate AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have been faithful' describes a completed state of faithfulness over a defined period ('over a few things'), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have been faithful is a stative predicate describing a persistent quality or condition over time, not a dynamic punctual event without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have been faithful' describes a state of faithfulness that holds over time without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, meeting the AktState criterion of a situation with no progression and qualitatively identical subintervals.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have been faithful over a few things' describes a factual situation that actually occurred (the servant was faithful), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'should have put' expresses a counterfactual conditional about an unrealized past action, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'should have put' expresses a counterfactual past obligation that did not occur, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.

DtExp_explanation: The target 'should have received' expresses a counterfactual conditional about what ought to have happened, not an assertion that the event occurred as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'should have received' expresses a counterfactual conditional about what ought to have happened, not an actual change of state that has occurred and holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'should have received' expresses a counterfactual past event that did not occur, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.

FullText: When Jesus had finished talking , he said to his disciples :

DtRes_explanation: The past perfect 'had finished talking' encodes a transition from the state of talking (State A) to the state of having completed talking (State B), with the resulting state holding at the reference time of the main clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had finished talking' expresses a completed action prior to the reference time, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had finished talking' presents the completion of the talking event as a bounded whole, with the transition to completion construed as a single accomplished fact prior to the main clause action.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had finished talking' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (a completed past action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired, so it does not instantiate TLMdIrr.

TLTS_explanation: The target 'could have been sold' instantiates TLTS because it encodes modal specification ('could') in addition to the perfect aspect ('have been'), distinguishing it from the simple present perfect form.

FullText: As he spoke , Judas , one of the twelve , followed by a crowd carrying swords and spears , approached him . The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them .

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had sent' describes a past event that occurred and is realized in reality, not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had sent' expresses a completed past action (the priests sent them before the approaching moment) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had sent' presents the sending as a completed, bounded event prior to the narrative moment, with no internal temporal development accessible, instantiating perfective aspect.

FullText: » This has taken place so that the writings of the prophets will come true . « Then all his disciples left him .

FullText: Then the high priest ripped his robes apart and said : » He has said evil against God ! Why do we need more witnesses ? You have heard his words of blasphemy ! «

FullText: While he was seated in judgment his wife sent a message to him . She said , » Do not judge that righteous man . I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him . «

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have had much trouble' expresses a state (trouble/distress) that began in the past (during the dream) and extends to the reference time (the moment of speaking), instantiating the DtUniv feature of continuous duration from past to present.

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have told' expresses a past action (telling the disciples) whose relevance extends to the reference time (the moment of speaking), instantiating the feature of a state holding from a past point to the present.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .

DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has been given' encodes a transition from a state where authority was not given to Jesus to a state where authority has been given to him, with this resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has been given' expresses a state (possession of authority) that began in the past and continues to hold at the reference time, matching the DtUniv feature definition.

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have commanded' indicates a past action (the commanding) whose resulting state (the commands being in effect) persists at the reference time, instantiating a change of state from non-commanded to commanded.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have commanded' expresses a past action (the commanding) with relevance extending to the reference time, indicating a state of things commanded that holds from past to present.

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' indicates a change of state from the time not having come to the time now being present, with this resulting state holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has come' expresses a completed action reaching a present state rather than a continuous state holding from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'have never seen' expresses a state of non-experience that persists from past to present, not a change of state from State A to State B with a resulting new state.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have never seen' expresses a negative state (not having seen) that holds continuously from the past up to the reference time of speaking, instantiating the DtUniv feature.

FullText: » If Satan has risen up against himself , he is divided , cannot stand and will come to an end .

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had done' is past perfect indicating a prior completed action relative to the past moment of looking, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.

DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has made you whole' encodes a change of state from a condition of affliction to wholeness, with the resulting state of being healed currently holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has made' describes a completed action with present relevance (the healing is now whole), not a state or activity that holds continuously from past to reference time.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has made you whole' is asserted as a realized fact within the speaker's utterance, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had become well known' entails a resulting change of state (transition from unknown to known) and is not punctual/instantaneous, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had become well known' describes a change from unknown to known state, involving dynamic progression toward an endpoint, which contradicts the stative definition requiring no inherent change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target describes a factual event that did occur (John the Baptist becoming well known), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.

DtExp_explanation: The target 'had become well known' presents the event of John becoming well known as part of his life history, answering whether this event occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had become well known' encodes a transition from a state of not being well known to a state of being well known, with the resulting state holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had become well known' expresses a state (being well known) that began at a past point and continues up to the reference time of the narrative, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had become well known' presents the transition to being well-known as a completed, bounded whole event without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had become well known' is asserted as a realized fact in the past (pluperfect aspect), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired, so it does not instantiate TLMdIrr.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had risen from the dead' entails a resulting change of state (transition from dead to alive), which violates the AktSemel requirement that there be no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had risen from the dead' describes a dynamic change with an inherent endpoint (the moment of resurrection), not a state that holds uniformly over time without progression.

DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'had risen from the dead' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had risen from the dead' encodes a transition from a dead state (State A) to a living state (State B), with the resulting state of being alive held at the reference time of King Herod's hearing about it.

FullText: Then he replied : » Thanks to your answer you may leave for the demon has gone out of your daughter . «

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had seen' is embedded in a reported command ('not to tell anyone what they had seen') and refers to a completed past action that was realized as fact, not a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: » Truly I tell you everywhere the good news is preached through out the whole world , that which this woman has done will be spoken of for a memorial of her . «

AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has done' describes a completed action (the woman's deed) that culminates in a defined result state (being spoken of as a memorial), instantiating the AktAcc feature of a dynamic event with inherent process leading to completion.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has done' presents a realized event (the woman's action) that occurred in reality, not an absent or non-occurring situation.

DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has done' indicates a completed action (the woman's deed) whose resulting state—that the deed is accomplished and will be remembered—holds at the reference time, instantiating a transition from not-done to done.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has done' expresses a completed past action with present relevance, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The phrase 'has done' presents the woman's action as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.

TLTS_explanation: The target 'has done' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'done') with no overt tense, mood, or aspect specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have been better' expresses a counterfactual conditional about an unrealized, hypothetical state contrary to fact, construing the situation as non-actual rather than asserted reality.

FullText: » The Son of man goes even as it is written of him . Woe to that man through whom the Son of man is betrayed ! It would have been better if he had not been born . «

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had not been born' is embedded in a counterfactual conditional ('It would have been better if he had not been born') that construes a non-actual, hypothetical situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.

FullText: He took a cup , and when he had given praise , he gave it to them and they all had a drink from it .

DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had given praise' describes a completed action prior to another past action ('he gave it to them'), not a resulting state that holds at the reference time; it is past perfect marking temporal sequence, not a state change with current relevance.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had given praise' expresses a completed past action (perfective aspect) that occurred before another past event ('he gave it to them'), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had given praise' presents the act of giving praise as a bounded, completed event prior to the main action ('he gave it to them'), construing it as a whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had given praise' is asserted as a realized fact within the narrative sequence of actual events (he took a cup, gave praise, gave it to them, they drank), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

FullText: He came a third time and said to them : » Sleep and take your rest . It is enough the hour has come . Look , the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners !

FullText: At the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , saying : » Eloi , Eloi , lama sabachthani ? « Interpreted it means , » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have forsaken' expresses a state (abandonment) that began in the past and continues to hold at the reference time of Jesus's utterance, matching the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have forsaken' presents the abandonment as a completed action with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition of being forsaken as a bounded whole without internal temporal development.

FullText: He bought a linen cloth , and taking him down , wound him in the linen cloth . Then he laid him in a tomb that had been cut out of a rock . He rolled a stone against the door of the tomb .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'cut' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (agent performing the cutting action) and an internal argument (the rock/tomb being affected), where the action is directed toward and modifies the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The passive construction 'had been cut' encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument (the tomb) functions as an internal argument (patient/theme), and the verb does not license an external argument in the surface structure.

AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'had been cut' describes a completed state of the tomb rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it is a resultant state in past perfect form, not the boundary point of transition itself.

DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had been cut' describes a completed action with a resulting state (the tomb exists in its cut form at the time of the main narrative), rather than presenting the event as part of the subject's life history without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had been cut' describes a completed action in the past with no indication that a resulting state holds at the reference time; it is a past perfect passive expressing prior completion, not a change of state with current effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been cut' describes a completed past action (the tomb was carved out before the reference time), not a state that holds continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had been cut' presents a prior completed state rather than a bounded transition itself; it describes the resultant condition of the tomb, not the cutting event as a single completed whole in the narrative foreground.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been cut' describes a completed past action (the tomb's creation) asserted as an actual realized fact within the narrative, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been cut' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'had been' + past participle 'cut'), the semantic patient 'tomb' functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: They went to the tomb when the sun had risen . It was very early .

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'when the sun had risen' describes a factual event that occurred before they went to the tomb, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.

DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had risen' encodes a change of state from the sun not having risen to the sun having risen, with the resulting state (sun risen) holding at the reference time when they went to the tomb.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had risen' expresses a completed action (the sun rising) that occurred before another past event (going to the tomb), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had risen' presents the rising of the sun as a bounded, completed event prior to the main action, treating the transition as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The past perfect 'had risen' describes a realized fact in the actual world (the sun's rising before they went to the tomb), not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had risen' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes a tense specification (past) other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an active voice construction where the sun (agent) is the grammatical subject performing the action of rising; there is no overt passive morphology, no by-phrase, and the semantic patient does not function as the grammatical subject.

FullText: He said to them : » Do not be amazed . Jesus the Nazarene , who has been impaled , is not here . He has risen . Behold , the place where they laid him !
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'impale' encodes a two-participant event with an external agent (implied crucifiers) and internal patient (Jesus), where the action is directed toward and affects the patient, satisfying the ASTrns feature.

AktState_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' describes a dynamic event (the action of impalement) with an inherent endpoint and change of state, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' describes a factual event that actually occurred (Jesus's crucifixion), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has been impaled' indicates a past action (crucifixion) whose resulting state (being dead/pierced) holds at the reference time, encoding a transition from alive to impaled that remains in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has been impaled' describes a completed past event (the crucifixion) with present relevance, not a state or activity that holds continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has been impaled' presents the crucifixion as a bounded, completed event (the transition from living to impaled state) without accessing its internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' is asserted as a realized fact about Jesus in the actual world (a past event that occurred), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a progressive perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + 'been' + past participle), which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has risen' indicates a change of state from a previous condition (being impaled/dead) to a new state (risen/alive) that holds at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has risen' expresses a state (Jesus being alive/risen) that began in the past and holds at the reference time of the utterance, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has risen' presents the transition of rising as a completed event with a bounded whole, making it perfective in aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has risen' is asserted as a realized fact within the speaker's discourse, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or non-actual.

FullText: They fled from the tomb , for trembling and astonishment had come on them . They said nothing to anyone for they were afraid .

DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'had come on them' indicates a change of state from a prior condition (without trembling and astonishment) to a new state (with trembling and astonishment now affecting them), with the resulting state holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'had come on them' expresses a completed past event (trembling and astonishment arriving upon them) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had come on' presents the transition of astonishment/trembling arriving as a bounded, completed event without internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had come on' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (trembling and astonishment actually came upon them), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

FullText: She told those who had been with him , for they mourned and wept .

DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had been with' presents the event of being with him as part of the subject's life history, answering whether they had experienced this state at least once, without asserting a current resulting state.

FullText: When they heard that he was alive and she had seen him they did not believe it .

DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once (she saw him) without asserting a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had seen' is a past perfect form indicating a prior completed action relative to the past reference time ('heard'), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at the reference time.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had seen' is a past perfect form describing a prior completed action relative to another past event ('he had risen'), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had seen' expresses a completed past action (those who witnessed the risen Jesus) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.

FullText: Afterward he was revealed to the eleven as they sat at the table . He rebuked them for their unbelief and hardness of heart , because they did not believe those who had seen him after he had risen .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant event with the subject 'he' as an external argument (agent/experiencer), not an internal argument, thus does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive but encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument (he) functions as the internal argument (theme/patient undergoing the rising action), not as an external argument/agent, violating the ASUnrg feature requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes a completed state change (resurrection) rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward completion; it is a perfective past event viewed as finished, not an unfolding process culminating in a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had risen' instantiates AktAch as it denotes a punctual change-of-state event (the crossing of the threshold from death to resurrection) rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: Had risen is a perfective past event with an inherent endpoint (completion of resurrection), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: Had risen describes a resulting change of state (transition from dead to alive/risen), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes a dynamic change (resurrection) with an inherent endpoint and progression, not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes an event that occurred and is realized as fact within the narrative, not an absent or non-occurring state.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'after he had risen' contains no overt adverbial explicitly anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'after' is a subordinating conjunction, not a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had risen' presents the resurrection as a past event that occurred at least once in the subject's (Jesus's) life history, answering whether the event happened without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had risen' encodes a change of state from not-risen to risen, with the resulting state (being risen) holding at the reference time of the narrative, satisfying the DtRes feature requirement.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had risen' expresses a completed past event (resurrection) that occurred before another past event (people seeing him), not a state holding continuously from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had risen' presents the resurrection as a bounded, completed event without internal temporal development, meeting the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had risen' is asserted as a realized fact (Jesus's resurrection) within the narrative, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had risen' is a past perfect form (pluperfect), which encodes a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an active voice construction where the subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the action of rising, not a patient; there is no by-phrase and no passive morphological marking.

FullText: After he had spoken to them the Lord Jesus , was received up into heaven , and sat down at the right hand of God .
