ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had appeared' presents the event as realized and occurring in past time, not as absent from reality; the clause reports what others said happened, without negation or polarity reversal.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had appeared' presents the event of Elijah's appearance as part of a life history (whether Elijah appeared at least once) without asserting that a resulting state currently holds, matching the DtExp feature definition.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had appeared' uses past perfect tense to indicate a prior completed event relative to the narrative past, not a change of state with resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had appeared' expresses a past event completed before another past reference point (what others said), not a continuous state or activity extending from past to present reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had appeared' presents the appearance as a bounded, completed event viewed as a whole from a past reference point, without accessing internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had appeared' is embedded in a reported speech context ('Others said that...') expressing what people claimed rather than asserting it as realized fact, making it non-actual/projected.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had appeared' is a past perfect form that encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had appeared' is active voice with Elijah as the semantic agent functioning as grammatical subject, not the semantic patient; there is no overt passive morphology or by-phrase present.

FullText: Others said that Elijah had appeared and others and that one of the old prophets had risen again .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had risen' is intransitive and encodes a one-participant event of movement/change of state directed by an internal argument (the prophet), not a two-participant transitive event with an external argument affecting an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a one-participant event with an external argument (the subject 'one of the old prophets'), not an internal argument as sole participant, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive and encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument 'one of the old prophets' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient undergoing change of state), not as an external argument/agent, thus does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes a state change (coming back to life) that reaches completion without necessarily implying an inherent process phase or durative unfolding toward a terminal point; it is a perfective achievement rather than an aktionsart with the dynamic, durative process-to-completion structure required by AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had risen' instantiates AktAch as it denotes a punctual change-of-state event (crossing the threshold from dead to alive) rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes a completed change of state (from dead to alive) with an inherent endpoint and result state, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Had risen entails a resulting change of state (from dead to alive), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had risen' describes a dynamic change from a previous state (not risen) to a new state (risen), with an inherent endpoint, which contradicts the AktState feature requiring a situation with no dynamic change and no inherent endpoint.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets had risen again' reports what others said as a claim or belief, not a counterfactual condition; it presents a possible event under the reported speech frame rather than explicitly marking it as contrary to fact.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had risen' presents the event of a prophet rising as part of the narrative's life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had risen' encodes a transition from a state of being dead to a state of being alive/resurrected, with the resulting state (risen/alive) holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had risen' expresses a past event completed before another past reference point (what others said), not a state or activity holding continuously from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had risen' presents the rising event as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had risen' is embedded in a reported speech context ('Others said that...') expressing what people claimed rather than asserting it as realized fact, making it non-actual/projected.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had risen' is a past perfect form that encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an active voice construction where the semantic agent ('one of the old prophets') functions as the grammatical subject, lacking the passive voice morphology and patient-as-subject structure required for VcPass.

FullText: When the apostles returned they told Jesus about the things they had done . They traveled to the city of Bethsaida .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'do' in 'had done' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the apostles as agent) and an internal argument (the things as patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the apostles toward and affecting those things.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' in 'had done' is a two-participant event where 'they' functions as an external argument (agent) performing the action, not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the things they had done), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' describes a completed action with a defined terminal point (the apostles' activities culminated in a finished state before they returned and reported), instantiating the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had done' expresses a completed action viewed as a whole rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold; it is a perfective aspect marking completion, not an achievement verb instantiating AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had done' describes a completed action with a defined endpoint (the apostles' activities are finished and reported), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' describes completed actions with resulting states (apostles' missionary work), not a punctual event without consequent result, so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had done' describes a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'had done' reports a past event that actually occurred (the apostles performed actions and then reported them), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had done' presents a realized event (the apostles' completed actions) as having occurred in reality, not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had done' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'When the apostles returned' is a temporal clause but does not provide a specific past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had done' presents the apostles' activities as part of their life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had done' is past perfect indicating a prior completed action before the apostles returned, not a change of state holding at the reference time; the resulting state does not persist into the present moment of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had done' expresses a completed action prior to the reference time (when apostles returned and told Jesus), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had done' reports a completed action that the apostles actually performed and then reported to Jesus, asserting it as a realized fact rather than as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had done' is a past perfect form that encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice with the apostles as the grammatical subject performing the action, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: They said : » John the Baptist . Others say Elijah and still others say one of the old prophets has risen again . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive with a single participant (the prophet) as the subject undergoing a change of state, not a two-participant transitive event with an external argument directing action toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' encodes a two-participant event where the external argument 'one of the old prophets' is the agent/experiencer and functions as the subject, not an internal argument, thus failing the ASUncc criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is intransitive but encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument (one of the old prophets) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient undergoing change of state), not as an external argument/agent, thus does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has risen' describes a completed state change (resurrection) rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a defined terminal point; it presents the result state without foregrounding the process of rising itself.
AktAch_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has risen' describes a state resulting from a past action rather than the punctual threshold-crossing moment itself, making it stative rather than an instantaneous change-of-state event.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has risen' describes a completed change of state (from dead to alive/resurrected) with an inherent endpoint and result state, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive/resurrected) with a resulting condition, not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has risen' describes a dynamic change (resurrection from death to life) with an inherent endpoint (the moment of rising), not a static situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' presents a reported belief or possibility, not a counterfactual statement contrary to fact; it describes what 'others say' without indicating the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has risen' presents the event as occurring or holding in reality (one of the old prophets has risen again), not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'again' indicates repetition but not a specific temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has risen' presents the event of a prophet rising as part of life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has risen' encodes a change of state from dead to alive (resurrection) with the resulting state of being risen held at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has risen' is a present perfect form expressing a past event (resurrection) with relevance continuing to the reference time, which instantiates the feature of a state holding from a past starting point up to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has risen' presents the transition of rising as a completed, bounded event without exposing its internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has risen again' is embedded within reported speech ('Others say...') expressing what people claim or believe rather than asserting it as an established fact, making it non-actual/hypothetical.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice with the semantic agent ('one of the old prophets') as the grammatical subject, lacking the passive voice morphology and patient-as-subject structure required for VcPass.

FullText: When the voice came Jesus was found alone . They kept quiet and told no man what they had seen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'see' in 'had seen' is a perception verb with an experiencer (internal argument) and a perceived entity (external argument), not a transitive action verb with an agent directing force toward a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' in 'had seen' is a two-participant event requiring an external argument (the experiencer 'they') and an internal argument (the theme of perception), thus it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an internal argument (the object of perception, implied by context), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' describes a completed perceptual event without an inherent process phase or terminal culmination point; it is perfective/punctual rather than dynamic and durative.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' describes a completed perceptual state rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing; it represents the result of a process (seeing) rather than the boundary point of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had seen' is a perfective past experience that describes a completed perceptual event with a defined endpoint, not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Had seen is a perfective past event that entails a resulting change of state (knowledge acquired), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' describes a completed perceptual event with a definite endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' describes a factual event that actually occurred (they witnessed something), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had seen' describes an event that occurred (they witnessed something), not an event that failed to occur or was absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had seen' presents the event of seeing as part of the disciples' life history, answering whether they had experienced this event at least once (witnessing Jesus's transfiguration or similar occurrence) without asserting a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had seen' is past perfect indicating a prior completed action relative to the narrative past, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had seen' expresses a completed past action (what they witnessed) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' presents the act of seeing as a bounded, completed event prior to the main narrative moment, treating it as a whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'what they had seen' refers to a realized past event (they actually witnessed something), not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had seen' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes a tense specification (past) other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent performing the action of seeing, not the patient; there is no overt passive morphology or by-phrase.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The lexical verb 'done' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (implied agent performing the works) and an internal argument (the mighty works as patient/theme affected by the action), construed as directed from agent toward the works.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' in passive voice encodes a one-participant event where 'mighty works' functions as the internal argument (theme/patient), and no external argument is licensed in the surface structure.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the mighty works as patient/theme), and the external agent is unexpressed/demoted in the passive construction, violating the requirement for a sole argument functioning as external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a stative passive construction describing a completed state rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The pluperfect passive 'had been done' describes a completed state or resultant condition rather than a punctual change-of-state event at a threshold boundary, making it non-achievement.
AktAct_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' describes a completed, punctual event with a defined result state (the accomplishment of miracles), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a resultant state (completion of works) rather than a punctual dynamic event, as it is in the passive perfect aspect indicating a prior action with lasting consequences, not a single bounded occurrence without result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed dynamic event (performance of mighty works) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had been done in Tyre and Sidon' is part of a counterfactual conditional structure ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented') that presents a contrary-to-fact situation: the works were not actually done in Tyre and Sidon, only in the addressed cities.
ClNegPol_explanation: The counterfactual conditional construction 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon' presents the event of mighty works occurring in those cities as not actually happening in reality, instantiating ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'had been done' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure and past perfect tense alone do not satisfy the requirement for an overt temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram is a counterfactual conditional (past perfect in a hypothetical clause) that does not assert the event occurred in the subject's life history, but rather presents a contrary-to-fact scenario, thus failing to instantiate DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a counterfactual conditional in a hypothetical scenario ('If the mighty works had been done...'), not indicating an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' expresses a past completed action in a counterfactual conditional, not a state holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had been done' presents the situation with internal temporal structure (completion prior to a reference point) rather than as a bounded whole, and does not construe a transition as a single completed event in the sense required by TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), which construes the situation as hypothetical and non-actual rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect passive mood (pluperfect), which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'had been' + past participle 'done'), the semantic patient 'mighty works' functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' is intransitive and encodes a one-participant event (internal change of mind/attitude) without an external agent directing action toward an affected patient, thus failing to instantiate the two-participant transitive structure required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repented' encodes a one-participant event but licenses an external argument (the subject 'they'), making it a two-participant structure that does not satisfy the ASUncc feature requirement of sole internal argument with no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' is a one-participant event, but the sole argument 'they' functions as the internal argument (experiencer/patient undergoing the mental state change), not as an external agent/initiator, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have repented' describes a dynamic event with an inherent process of changing one's behavior that culminates in a defined terminal state of repentance, satisfying the AktAcc feature requirement.
AktAch_explanation: Repentance is a change-of-state event representing a transition from one moral/spiritual condition to another, conceived as a punctual threshold crossing rather than an extended process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a completed action with a defined endpoint (the state of having repented), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Would have repented denotes a change of state (from not repenting to repenting) with a resulting condition, not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have repented' describes a dynamic change of state (from not repenting to repenting) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under contrary-to-fact conditions (if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon), indicating an event understood not to have occurred in the actual world.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the overt adverbial 'long ago' which explicitly anchors the counterfactual repentance event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened in an unrealized hypothetical scenario, not an event presented as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not a change of state that has occurred and holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have repented' expresses a counterfactual past event with a definite completion point ('long ago'), not a continuous state from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional perfect construction 'would have repented' presents a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than a bounded completed event, focusing on the counterfactual condition rather than the event as a perfective whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The conditional structure 'If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented' construes the repentance as a hypothetical, counterfactual situation that did not actually occur, not as a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have repented' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a modal specification (conditional 'would') combined with perfect aspect, which is a tense/mood specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is an active voice construction where the semantic agent (they/people of Tyre and Sidon) functions as the grammatical subject, lacking the passive voice morphology (be + past participle) and agent-demotion structure required for VcPass.

FullText: » Behold , I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions , and over all the power of the enemy : and nothing shall in any way hurt you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'give' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (I/agent) directing an action toward and affecting an internal argument (you/patient receiving authority), satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have given' encodes a two-participant event (giver and recipient) with 'you' as an external argument (recipient/beneficiary), not a one-participant event with a sole internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have given' licenses two internal arguments ('you' as recipient and 'authority' as theme), violating the ASUnrg requirement of no internal arguments.
AktAcc_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have given' denotes a completed action with a resultant state (possession of authority), representing a dynamic event that culminates in a defined terminal point where the authority transfer is finished and the recipient now possesses it.
AktAch_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have given' represents a completed change-of-state event where authority transitions from not-possessed to possessed, marking a definable threshold crossing at a punctual moment.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have given (you) authority' is a perfective accomplishment with a defined result state (authority transferred), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have given (you) authority' entails a resulting change of state (the recipient now possesses authority), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'have given you authority' describes a completed action with a resultant state, not a situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression, as it involves a transfer event with an inherent endpoint.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' describes a factual assertion of an action that has occurred in the actual world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have given (you) authority' presents the event as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated, thus does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; while 'have given' is present perfect tense, there is no adverbial modifier like 'already', 'just', or a temporal expression that anchors it to a particular past moment.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have given (you) authority' presents a past event (giving of authority) as part of the subject's action history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds, matching the DtExp feature definition.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have given' indicates a completed action with a resulting state (you now possess authority) that holds at the reference time, matching the DtRes feature definition of a transition from State A to State B with the new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have given' expresses a completed past action with present relevance, not a continuous state holding from past to reference time as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have given' presents the act of granting authority as a completed, bounded whole event with relevance to the present moment, consistent with perfective aspect construal.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'I have given you authority' is asserted as a completed action in the actual world, not as a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have given' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt encoding of tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have given you authority' is active voice with 'I' as the agent subject, 'you' as the recipient, and 'authority' as the direct object; the patient does not function as the grammatical subject, and there is no passive morphological marking.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . No one knows who the Son is except the Father . And no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son is willing to reveal him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The passive voice construction 'have been delivered' does not instantiate ASTrns because the lexical verb 'deliver' lacks an overt external argument (agent) in this clause, and the internal argument 'things' functions as the grammatical subject rather than as a directed object of transitive action.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'delivered' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'my Father' who performs the delivering), so it does not meet the criterion of encoding a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' is a passive construction with an internal argument ('All things'), not a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have been delivered' describes a dynamic process of transfer that culminates in a defined terminal point (completion of delivery), with an inherent process phase leading toward the result state of things being in the possession of the recipient.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' describes a completed transfer of possession rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold; it emphasizes the resultant state of having been given rather than the instantaneous moment of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' describes a completed transfer with a result state (things now possessed by the recipient), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things now possessed by the subject), which violates the AktSemel requirement that excludes consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the transfer of things from Father to Son), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All things have been delivered to me from my Father' describes an actual state of affairs presented as factual, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' presents the event as realized and occurring in reality (all things have actually been delivered), not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' asserts that a resulting state (all things being in the possession of the subject) currently holds, rather than merely recording whether the event occurred at least once in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' indicates a completed action (delivery of all things) with the resulting state (things now being in the possession of the speaker) holding at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the DtUniv criterion of continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have been delivered' presents the delivery as a completed bounded event (transition from not-delivered to delivered state) without exposing internal temporal development, instantiating TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have been delivered' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a progressive perfect form (present perfect + passive voice with 'been'), which is a specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' instantiates VcPass: the semantic patient 'All things' functions as grammatical subject, the agent 'my Father' is expressed in a by-phrase (implied in 'from my Father'), and overt passive morphology is present in the auxiliary 'have been' + past participle 'delivered'.

FullText: » However one thing is needed . Mary has chosen the proper way and it shall not be taken away from her . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'chosen' encodes a two-participant event with Mary as external argument (agent/initiator) and 'the proper way' as internal argument (patient/theme), where the choosing is directed from Mary toward and affects the way selected.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'chosen' licenses an external argument (Mary as agent/subject) and an internal argument (the proper way as theme/patient), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'chosen' licenses an internal argument ('the proper way'), so it does not encode a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase leading toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it represents the result of a decision process, not the instantaneous crossing of a threshold.
AktAct_explanation: "has chosen" is a perfective aspect expressing a completed action with a result state (Mary's selection is finished), not a dynamic durative situation unfolding without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has chosen' instantiates a resultative perfect aspect that entails a resulting change of state (Mary's selection now stands as a completed decision with present relevance), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result condition as required by AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' describes a completed dynamic action with a resultant state, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Mary has chosen the proper way' describes a factual event that has occurred in the actual world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has chosen' presents Mary's action as a realized event that occurred in reality, not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has chosen' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' presents Mary's choice as a completed event in her life history without asserting that a resulting state currently holds, answering whether the event has occurred at least once.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' indicates a completed action (Mary's choice) with a resulting state (her having chosen the proper way) that holds at the reference time, exemplifying a transition from not-having-chosen to having-chosen.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' expresses a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state or activity extending from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has chosen' presents the act of choosing as a bounded, completed event without accessing its internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has chosen' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (Mary's past action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has chosen' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'chosen') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has chosen' is active voice with Mary as the grammatical subject and agent performing the action, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: » If I use the finger power Spirit of God to cast out demons , then the kingdom of God has come upon you ?
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' is intransitive and does not encode a two-participant event with an external agent directing action toward an affected internal argument; rather, it describes the kingdom of God arriving at a location with 'upon you' as a locative modifier, not a patient argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the kingdom of God as subject/agent), making it a two-participant event that does not fit the one-participant internal-argument-only requirement of ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument 'upon you' (locative/goal), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' denotes a dynamic event (arrival of God's kingdom) that unfolds through a process and culminates in a defined terminal point (the kingdom's arrival upon the addressee), instantiating AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has come upon you' instantiates AktAch as it denotes a punctual change-of-state event where the kingdom of God crosses a definable threshold from not-present to present, conceived as a boundary point of transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' denotes a completed action with a resultant state (arrival of the kingdom), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' denotes a resultative state (the kingdom's arrival and presence) rather than a punctual dynamic event without consequent result, as it entails a lasting change of state from the kingdom not being present to being present.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' describes a dynamic change with an inherent endpoint (arrival of the kingdom), not a state that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the kingdom of God has come upon you' presents a factual consequence that would occur if the conditional antecedent is true, not a situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has come (upon you)' presents the kingdom of God as realized and occurring, not as absent from reality, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has come' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional clause provides logical anchoring but not temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'has come upon you' asserts that the kingdom of God has arrived and is present, expressing a resulting state that currently holds rather than merely recording whether an event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' indicates a change of state (the kingdom of God transitioning from not-present to present) with the resulting state (the kingdom being upon you) holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' expresses a completed action with present relevance (arrival of the kingdom), not a continuous state or activity holding from past to present as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' presents the arrival of God's kingdom as a bounded, completed transition without internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has come' is asserted as a realized fact within the conditional clause, not construed as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'come') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come upon you' is active voice with 'kingdom of God' as the grammatical subject performing the action, not a passive construction where the semantic patient functions as the grammatical subject.

FullText: » Woe to you Pharisees ! You tithe mint and rue and every herb , and pass over justice and the love of God . You should have done these but you leave them undone .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' encodes a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (implicit 'you') as agent and an internal argument ('these' referring to justice and love of God) as patient/theme, with the event construed as directed from agent toward and affecting the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you'), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' takes an internal argument 'these' (object), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' describes a dynamic, durative action of performing/accomplishing specific duties (justice and love of God) that would necessarily culminate in a defined result state of completion, fitting the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' expresses a counterfactual obligation or expectation rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' describes a completed action with a defined goal (doing justice and love of God), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' expresses a counterfactual obligation with a resulting state condition (things left undone), not a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' expresses a counterfactual obligation about a completed action with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'should have done these but you leave them undone' presents a counterfactual situation where the Pharisees ought to have performed an action (doing justice and loving God) but did not, indicating a contrast between what should have occurred and what actually occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' presents the action of doing justice and love of God as not occurring in reality, as confirmed by the following clause 'but you leave them undone,' which explicitly negates the realization of the prescribed action.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; it expresses a counterfactual obligation without temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' expresses a counterfactual obligation about what ought to have occurred, not an assertion that the event actually happened as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' expresses a counterfactual obligation about a past action that was not performed, not a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual past obligation that was not fulfilled, not a continuous state holding from past to reference time as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The construction 'should have done these' presents a completed action as a bounded whole event (the doing of justice and love of God), construing it as a single perfective situation that ought to have been accomplished.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The construction 'should have done' expresses a counterfactual or unrealized obligation—what ought to have happened but did not—projecting a non-actual situation contrary to the realized fact that 'you leave them undone.'
TLTS_explanation: The target 'should have done' encodes modal specification (should) in addition to the perfect aspect (have + past participle), which distinguishes it from the simple present perfect and thus instantiates the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'should have done (these)' is active voice with an implicit agent (you), not passive voice; the semantic patient 'these' functions as the grammatical object, not subject, and there is no morphological passive marking.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'said' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (you as agent/speaker) and an internal argument (the utterance as theme), where the event is directed from the speaker toward the content being expressed.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'said' is a two-participant event licensing an external argument (the implicit speaker) and an internal argument (the content said), so it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have said' licenses an internal argument (the content of what was said, 'that which you have said'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have said' describes a completed past action with present relevance rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have said' is a perfect aspect form describing a completed state of speech rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a threshold, thus does not instantiate AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have said' is a perfective construction indicating a completed speech act with a defined endpoint (the utterance), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have said' entails a resulting change of state (words uttered in darkness will be heard in light), making it a perfective accomplishment rather than a punctual dynamic event without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have said' describes a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint (the act of speaking), not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have said' describes an actual event that occurred (something was said in darkness), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have said' presents a realized event (something that was actually said in the darkness), not an absent or non-occurring situation, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'you have said' contains no overt adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'in the darkness' modifies where the saying occurred, not when.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have said' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have said' indicates a past action (saying in darkness) with a resulting state (the words now exist as having been said) that holds at the reference time, encoding a transition from not-said to said.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have said' expresses a past completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the DtUniv criterion of continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have said' presents the speech act as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, meeting the perfective aspect criterion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have said' is asserted as a realized fact (something actually said in darkness), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have said' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have said' is active voice with the agent 'you' as grammatical subject and lacks passive morphological marking; the passive construction appears later in 'will be heard' and 'will be proclaimed'.

FullText: » That which you have said in the darkness will be heard in the light . That which you have spoken in the inner chambers will be proclaimed on the housetops .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'spoken' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the implicit agent 'you') directing an action toward an internal argument (the utterance/words), meeting the ASTrns criterion of a transitive event directed from agent toward affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'spoken' encodes a two-participant event (speaker and addressee/content) with an external argument (the implicit agent 'you'), not a one-participant internal-argument-only event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'spoken' licenses an internal argument (the implied object of speech, 'that which you have spoken'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have spoken' describes a completed past action with present relevance, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase unfolding toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: Have spoken is a perfect aspect form describing a completed state rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it does not represent the boundary point of transition itself but rather the resultant state of a prior speaking action.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is a perfective aspect expressing a completed action with a defined endpoint (the act of speaking), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have spoken denotes a completed action with a resulting state (words that were spoken remain spoken), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is a perfective aspect form indicating a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'have spoken' describes a past action that actually occurred (something was spoken in inner chambers), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have spoken' presents a realized event (something that has actually occurred), not an absent or non-occurring state, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'you have spoken in the inner chambers' contains no overt adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'in the inner chambers' is a locative prepositional phrase, not a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'have spoken' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state, which instantiates the DtExp feature.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have spoken' indicates a past action (speaking in inner chambers) whose resulting state of having-been-spoken holds at the reference time, encoding a transition from not-having-spoken to having-spoken with the new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have spoken' expresses a past action with present relevance (present perfect) rather than a continuous state from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have spoken' presents the speech act as a completed, bounded whole event without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is asserted as a realized fact (past action completed in the actual world), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have spoken' is active voice with the agent 'you' as the grammatical subject; it does not instantiate VcPass because the patient does not function as the grammatical subject and there is no passive morphological marking.

FullText: » I will show you the one to fear . Fear the one who , after he has killed , has power to destroy you throw into the ever-burning trash fires of the valley of Hinnom Greek : Gehenna . This is one you should fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'killed' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (agent who performs the killing) and an internal argument (patient/theme affected by the action), directed from agent toward patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has killed' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the implicit agent 'he') and an internal argument (the implicit patient/theme being killed), thus it licenses an external argument and does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'killed' licenses an internal argument (the patient/object being killed, though unexpressed), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has killed' describes a completed past action without inherent durative process or terminal culmination point in the present moment, failing to instantiate the dynamic, durative event with defined terminal point required by AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The perfect aspect 'has killed' instantiates AktAch by presenting the completion of a killing event as a punctual change-of-state that crosses the threshold from alive to dead, with focus on the resultant state rather than the process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has killed' describes a completed action with a defined endpoint and result state (death), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint, thus does not instantiate AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has killed' entails a resulting change of state (the victim is dead) and describes a completed action with consequent results, rather than a punctual event without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has killed' describes a completed dynamic action with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after he has killed' describes a factual conditional scenario (if someone kills, then that person has power to destroy), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has killed' describes a realized event (the killing has occurred) rather than an absent or non-occurring state, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has killed' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'after' is a conjunction introducing a temporal clause, not an adverbial modifier of the killing event itself.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has killed' presents the killing as a completed event in the subject's life history without asserting a current resulting state, directly instantiating the DtExp feature.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has killed' instantiates DtRes by indicating a completed action (killing) whose resulting state (the victim being dead) persists at the reference time of the conditional clause.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has killed' expresses a completed past action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the DtUniv criterion of continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has killed' presents the killing as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present moment, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'after he has killed' describes a completed action within a conditional/relative clause about a feared person, asserting a realized fact about that person's capability rather than projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has killed' is a canonical present perfect form (has + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has killed' is active voice with the agent 'he' as the grammatical subject and the patient unexpressed; it lacks the passive voice morphology and patient-as-subject structure required for VcPass.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'know' in 'had known' is a cognitive/mental state verb that takes an internal argument (the proposition about the thief's arrival) but lacks the two-participant transitive structure with an external agent directing force toward an affected patient that characterizes ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'known' licenses an external argument (the master as the knower) and takes an internal complement ('in what hour the thief was coming'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'known' licenses an internal argument (the complement 'in what hour the thief was coming'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had known' is a stative verb in the past perfect conditional expressing a hypothetical state of knowledge, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had known' expresses a hypothetical past state of knowledge rather than a dynamic change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold, thus does not instantiate AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had known' is a perfective past conditional expressing a completed mental state with a defined endpoint (acquiring knowledge), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had known' is a stative verb in the pluperfect expressing a resulting state of knowledge rather than a punctual dynamic event, and it entails a change of state (from not knowing to knowing), which violates the AktSemel feature definition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had known' expresses a hypothetical past state with a definite endpoint (the moment of knowing), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'if the master of the house had known' presents a counterfactual condition: the master did not actually know the hour of the thief's coming, yet the conditional structure with 'had known' and the consequent 'would have watched' describes what would have occurred under different (contrary-to-fact) circumstances.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had known' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional clause lacks a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had known' is a counterfactual conditional (contrary-to-fact) that does not present an event as part of the subject's actual life history, but rather as a hypothetical unrealized scenario.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had known' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had known' expresses a counterfactual past condition, not a state holding continuously from a past point to reference time; it describes a hypothetical prior knowledge that did not occur.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had known' presents the hypothetical knowing as a bounded, completed event prior to the conditional moment, treating the cognitive transition as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had known' is part of a counterfactual conditional structure ('if the master...had known...he would have watched') that construes a non-actual, hypothetical situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had known' is a past perfect form (pluperfect), which encodes a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is an active voice construction where the master (subject) is the agent performing the action of knowing, not the patient; there is no overt passive morphology or by-phrase indicating passive voice.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'watched' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the master/agent) directing attention toward and monitoring an internal argument (the house/theme), satisfying the ASTrns feature.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'watched' encodes a two-participant event where the subject (he/master) is an external argument (agent) performing the action, not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an internal argument (the implicit object of surveillance, the house/property being monitored), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have watched' instantiates AktAcc as a dynamic, durative event with an inherent process phase (the act of watching) that culminates in a defined terminal point (completion of the watching period before the thief arrives).
AktAch_explanation: Would have watched describes a sustained vigilant state rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing, making it incompatible with the AktAch feature.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have watched' expresses a hypothetical conditional action with an implicit endpoint (preventing the thief's entry), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Would have watched entails a resulting change of state (the house would not have been broken into), making it a consequential action rather than a punctual event without result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have watched' describes a dynamic action with inherent progression and an endpoint (watching until the thief arrives), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The conditional structure 'if the master...had known...he would have watched' presents a counterfactual scenario where the master did not actually know when the thief was coming, and therefore did not actually watch, making this contrary to fact.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would have watched' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'in what hour' modifies 'coming' not the target verb.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under unrealized circumstances, not a past event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have watched' expresses a counterfactual conditional past action (what would have occurred if a condition had been met), not a continuous state or activity extending from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' presents the hypothetical action as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on the unmet condition 'if the master of the house had known,' thus instantiating the TLMdIrr feature.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have watched' encodes a modal specification (conditional 'would') combined with the perfect aspect, which is a tense/mood specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent performing the action of watching, not a patient receiving the action.

FullText: » Know this , that if the master of the house had known in what hour the thief was coming he would have watched . He would not have allowed his house to be broken into .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'allowed' encodes a two-participant event where the master (external argument/agent) directs an action toward his house (internal argument/patient), with the event construed as affecting the patient through permission or prevention of breaking in.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses an external argument (the master as the subject who permits or prevents), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'allowed' licenses an internal argument ('his house to be broken into'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target instantiates AktAcc as it describes a dynamic, durative preventive action (not allowing/preventing) that necessarily culminates in a defined result state (the house remaining unbroken into), with an inherent process phase of vigilance leading toward that completion.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' expresses a counterfactual permission/prevention state rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' expresses a counterfactual prevention with a defined endpoint (stopping the breaking in), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' entails a resulting change of state (prevention of house-breaking) and describes a durative control action rather than a punctual, instantaneous event.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' expresses a counterfactual conditional action with an inherent endpoint (preventing a specific event), involving dynamic change and agency, which does not instantiate the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'would not have allowed his house to be broken into' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what the master would have done under different conditions (if he had known when the thief was coming), implying he did not actually prevent the break-in in reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would not have allowed' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram 'would not have allowed' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under hypothetical circumstances, not a past event presented as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target gram 'would not have allowed' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened in an unrealized past scenario, not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have allowed' expresses a counterfactual conditional contrary-to-fact scenario, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to reference time.

FullText: » Your brother returned home , the servant answered . Your father killed the prize calf because he has returned safely .

FullText: » He spoke to his father in anger . All these years I have worked like a slave for you . I have never disobeyed you . What have you given me ? Not even a goat for me to have a feast with my friends !

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have worked' expresses a continuous state of labor extending from a past starting point ('All these years') up to the reference time, instantiating DtUniv.

FullText: He said to him : » Every man sets out the good wine first . When the men have drunk freely the lower quality wine is served . You have kept the good wine until now . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'kept' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (You) as agent/initiator and an internal argument (the good wine) as patient/theme, where the action is directed from the agent toward the patient and affects it (retention/preservation).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'kept' is transitive and licenses an external argument (You), making it a two-participant event where the subject is an external argument (agent), not an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'kept' licenses an internal argument ('the good wine'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'have kept' denotes a durative action (keeping/maintaining) that has progressed from a past point until now and culminates in the present result state of the wine remaining in good quality.
AktAch_explanation: Have kept is a perfect aspect form describing a state resulting from a past action, not a punctual change-of-state event crossing a threshold.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have kept' presents the event of keeping good wine as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'have kept' indicates a change of state from not having kept the good wine (earlier) to having kept it (now), with the resulting state of possession/preservation holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have kept' expresses a state (possession/retention of good wine) that has held continuously from a past starting point up to the reference time ('until now'), matching the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have kept' presents the action of keeping the wine as a bounded, completed state with relevance to the present moment ('until now'), construing the retention as a single accomplished whole rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have kept' is asserted as a realized fact about the addressee's actual action (you have kept the good wine until now), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have kept' is a canonical present perfect form with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: » This is the truth . We speak what we know and witness about what we have seen . But you do not receive our testimony .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'see' in 'have seen' is a perception verb with an external argument (experiencer) and internal argument (perceived entity), but the event is not construed as directed action affecting the object; rather, it encodes passive perception or experience, which does not satisfy the ASTrns requirement of directed action toward and affecting an internal argument.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have seen' presents a realized event (witnessing that actually occurred), not an absent or non-occurring state, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have seen' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'what we have seen' presents the event of seeing as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' indicates a change of state from not having witnessed something to having witnessed it, with the resulting state of possession of that knowledge holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' expresses a state of knowledge resulting from past experience that extends to the reference time, matching the DtUniv feature of continuous holding from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' presents a past event with current relevance rather than as a bounded whole completed event; it makes the internal temporal development accessible through the connection between past action and present state, which does not align with the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'what we have seen' is asserted as a realized fact about actual witnessing and knowledge, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have seen' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have seen' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'we' as the semantic agent performing the action of seeing, not a patient functioning as subject with overt passive morphology.

FullText: » No one has ever ascended into heaven but the Son of man who descended from heaven .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'ascended' encodes a one-participant event of motion/change of location with only an internal argument (the Son of man), lacking the two-participant agent-patient structure where an external argument directs force toward affecting an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'ascended' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'one/the Son of man'), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'ascended' licenses an internal argument (the PP 'into heaven'), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with no internal argument.

AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has ascended into heaven' represents a dynamic change-of-state event where the subject crosses a definable threshold (from earthly to heavenly realm), conceived as a completed punctual transition rather than an unfolding process.

AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has ascended into heaven' describes a dynamic change involving progression from one location to another, with an inherent endpoint (arrival in heaven), which contradicts the AktState feature requiring a situation that holds over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'No one has ever ascended into heaven but the Son of man' presents an actual fact about reality (that only the Son of man has ascended), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has ascended' presents an event that is realized and occurring (the Son of man has ascended), not absent from reality; the negation applies only to others ('No one...but'), not to the target gram itself.
