DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has ascended' presents the ascension as an event that occurred at least once in the subject's (the Son of man's) life history, answering whether the event has taken place without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has ascended' indicates a change of state from not-ascended to ascended, with the resulting state (being in heaven) holding at the reference time, which matches the DtRes feature definition.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has ascended' expresses a state (the Son of man being in heaven) that holds continuously from a past starting point (the ascension event) up to the reference time, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has ascended' presents the ascension as a bounded, completed transition event without exposing its internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has ascended' is asserted as a realized fact about the Son of man in the actual world, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has ascended' is a canonical present perfect form (have/has + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has ascended' is active voice (the subject 'one' performs the action of ascending), not passive voice, as there is no patient functioning as grammatical subject and no agent expressed in a by-phrase or omitted passive construction.

FullText: » He who has an active faith in him is not judged . He who does not have an active faith has been judged already for he has not had faith in the name of the only begotten Son of God .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'judged' encodes a two-participant event with an implicit external argument (judge/agent) and an internal argument (the person being judged as patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed toward and affecting the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'judged' licenses an external argument (implicit agent 'God' or a judge), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant event with only an internal argument.

AktAch_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been judged' represents a completed change-of-state event where judgment has crossed a definable threshold from non-judged to judged status, instantiating AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been judged' is a perfective passive construction indicating a completed action with a resultant state (being judged), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has been judged' expresses a resulting state of judgment rather than a punctual dynamic event, as it describes a consequent condition (being in a judged state) rather than a single bounded occurrence without result.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has been judged' describes a completed action with a resultant state, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'has been judged' describes an actual state of affairs in the world presented by the text (those without faith are judged), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has been judged' presents the state of being judged as an actual realized condition for those lacking active faith, which instantiates ClNegPol by construing a negative evaluation or condemnation as holding true for the subject.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has been judged' contains the overt adverbial 'already' which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'has been judged' presents the event of being judged as part of the subject's life history, answering whether judgment has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'has been judged' encodes a change of state from not-judged to judged, with the resulting state (being judged) holding at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has been judged' expresses a completed past action with present relevance (present perfect), not a continuous state from a past starting point to the reference time as required by DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'has been judged' presents judgment as a completed, bounded event with a resultant state (already judged), construing the transition to judgment as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has been judged' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating a completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been judged' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'has been' + past participle 'judged'), the semantic patient 'He who does not have an active faith' functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » He who has an active faith in him is not judged . He who does not have an active faith has been judged already for he has not had faith in the name of the only begotten Son of God .

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has not had faith' describes a state or condition of lacking faith over time, not a dynamic punctual event, and entails a resulting change of state (being judged already), thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has not had faith (in)' describes a state of lacking faith that holds over time without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, where any subinterval is qualitatively identical to the whole, matching the AktState feature definition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target describes a factual state in the actual world (the person has not had faith), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has not had faith (in)' explicitly negates the occurrence of the state of having faith, presenting faith as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'has not had faith (in)' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'has not had faith (in)' presents the event of having faith as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'has not had faith' encodes a resultant state where the absence of faith is the current condition resulting from a prior transition, satisfying the DtRes feature requirement of a change of state with the resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has not had faith' expresses a negative state at a past point (already judged), not a continuous state from past to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has not had faith' presents the lack of faith as a bounded, completed state with relevance to the present moment, construing the absence of faith as a single whole rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has not had faith (in)' is asserted as a realized fact explaining why someone 'has been judged already,' not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has not had faith' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a past perfect form ('had' as past participle) rather than the canonical present perfect, marking a tense specification beyond simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has not had faith (in)' is an active voice construction where the subject 'he' is the agent performing the action of not having faith, not the patient receiving an action, and there is no passive morphological marking or by-phrase present.

FullText: » This is the judgment , that the light has come into the world . People loved the darkness rather than the light for their works were evil .

AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has come' describes a change of state (light entering the world) with a resulting condition, not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has come' presents the light as having entered the world as a realized, positive event, not as absent or negated from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the light has come into the world' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' presents the light's arrival as an event that occurred in the past and is part of the world's history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has come' encodes a transition from a state where light had not come into the world to a state where light has now come into the world, with this resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' expresses a past event (the light coming into the world) with relevance extending to the reference time, indicating a state that holds from that past point to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has come' presents the transition of light coming into the world as a completed, bounded event without exposing its internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has come' asserts the light's arrival as a realized fact in the actual world, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'come') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: John had not yet been thrown into prison .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'throw' encodes a two-participant event with an external agent (implied thrower) and internal patient (John), where the action is directed toward and affects John, satisfying the ASTrns feature despite passive voice.
ASUncc_explanation: The target is a passive construction where 'throw' is a two-participant verb licensing an external argument (agent), so it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target is a passive construction where 'throw' is a two-participant event (agent/thrower and patient/thrown entity); John functions as the internal argument (patient), not the external argument (agent), violating the ASUnrg requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The target instantiates AktAcc because 'throw into prison' is a dynamic, durative event with an inherent process phase (the action of throwing) that necessarily culminates in a defined terminal point (the result state of being in prison), even though the negation indicates non-occurrence.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had not been thrown' instantiates AktAch because 'thrown into prison' represents a punctual change-of-state event—the crossing of a definable threshold from freedom to imprisonment—conceived as a boundary point of transition rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target is a perfective passive construction describing a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (the state of being in prison), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The target describes a resulting change of state (transition into prison), which violates the AktSemel requirement that there be no resulting change of state; additionally, the negation and perfect aspect indicate a non-instantaneous temporal frame rather than a punctual event.
AktState_explanation: The target describes a dynamic event (throwing into prison) that involves inherent progression and a definite endpoint, which contradicts the stative nature required by AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target describes a factual state in the actual world (John has not been thrown into prison) rather than a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not been thrown' explicitly negates the event of being thrown into prison, presenting the situation as not occurring or not realized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the overt adverbial 'yet' which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval by indicating the event had not occurred up to that point in the past.
DtExp_explanation: The target gram 'had not been thrown' presents the event as part of John's life history, answering whether being thrown into prison has occurred (negatively), without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target gram 'had not been thrown' encodes a negated state change where the transition to being in prison has NOT occurred, so no resulting state of imprisonment holds at the reference time, failing to meet the DtRes requirement of a change with resulting state in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target gram 'had not been thrown' expresses a negative perfect state (non-occurrence up to a point in time) rather than a continuous state or activity holding from a past starting point to the reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had not been thrown' is negated and presents an unrealized situation rather than a bounded completed event; the perfective aspect requires a realized transition construed as a single whole, which negation prevents.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target gram 'had not been thrown' is asserted as a realized fact about John's actual state (he was not in prison at that point in time), not as a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had not been thrown' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect tense (marked by 'had'), which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been thrown' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'been' + past participle 'thrown'), the semantic patient 'John' functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » You bear witness that I said I am not the Christ . I have been sent before him .

AktState_explanation: The target 'have been sent' describes a completed action with a resultant state, involving dynamic change and progression from not-sent to sent, which contradicts the stative, unchanging nature required by AktState.

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have been sent' indicates a change of state from not-sent to sent, with the resulting state (being sent) holding at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have been sent' is asserted as a realized fact about the speaker's actual mission/purpose, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has seen' describes a state of accumulated experience rather than a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing, failing to meet the AktAch criterion.

FullText: » The Father does not judge any man for he has given all judgment to the Son .

DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' expresses a past action (the Father's giving of judgment to the Son) with relevance extending to the reference time, indicating a state that holds continuously from that past point to the present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' presents the act of giving judgment as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present state, instantiating perfective aspect by construing the transition as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has given all judgment to the Son' is asserted as a realized fact about what the Father has done, not as a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.

FullText: » Truly , Truly I say to you , he who hears my word , and believes him that sent me , has everlasting life . He will not receive judgment for he has passed out of death into life .

AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death' instantiates AktAch as it represents a punctual change-of-state event marking the crossing of a definable threshold from death into life.

AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' entails a resulting change of state (transition from death to life), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.

DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' presents a resultant state (currently being in life, no longer in death) rather than merely asserting that an event occurred in the subject's life history without regard to its current relevance.
DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' encodes a transition from State A (death) to State B (life), with the resulting state (life) holding at the reference time, matching the DtRes feature definition.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has passed out of death' expresses a completed transition from one state to another (death to life), not a continuous state holding from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'has passed out of death' presents the transition from death to life as a bounded, completed whole event without internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has passed out of death into life' is asserted as a realized fact about the believer, not as a projected or hypothetical situation.

VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has passed out of death' is active voice with 'he' as the grammatical subject performing the action of passing, not a passive construction where a patient would be the subject with an agent in a by-phrase or omitted.

FullText: » You sent people to John the Baptist and he has witnessed about the truth .

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has witnessed' describes a completed action with a resulting state of knowledge/testimony about the truth, which entails a consequent result condition rather than being a punctual event without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has witnessed' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the act of witnessing), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.

DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has witnessed' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect; it reports what John the Baptist has done (testified), not a transition from one state to another that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has witnessed' expresses a state or activity (witnessing about the truth) that began in the past and continues to hold relevance up to the reference time, instantiating the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has witnessed' presents the act of witnessing as a bounded, completed event without accessing its internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has witnessed' is asserted as a realized fact about John the Baptist's actual testimony regarding the truth, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

FullText: » The testimony I have is greater than that of John . The works I do are the works the Father gave me to accomplish . They testify that the Father has sent me .

AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has sent' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the sending event), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has sent' presents the event of the Father sending as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the Father has sent me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has sent' presents the event of the Father sending the speaker as part of the subject's life history, answering whether this event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has sent' indicates a completed action (sending) with a resulting state (the Father having sent me) that holds at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has sent' expresses a completed past action (the Father sent me) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has sent' presents the sending as a completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition of sending as a bounded whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has sent me' is asserted as a realized fact about the Father's action, not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has sent' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'sent') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .

DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' presents a negative experiential event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event (hearing the Father's voice) has occurred at least once, without asserting a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' encodes a change of state from a prior state of potentially hearing to the current state of not having heard, with this negative resultant state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The gram 'have not heard' expresses a negative state that has held continuously from a past point (when the Father's voice could have been heard) up to the reference time (the present moment of speaking), instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have not heard' presents a state of non-completion extending to the present moment, making internal temporal development accessible, rather than bounding the situation as a completed whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have not heard' is asserted as an actual fact about the addressees' realized experience, not as a projected or hypothetical situation.

FullText: » The Father who sent me testifies about me . You have not heard his voice nor seen him at any time .

DtExp_explanation: The gram 'have seen' presents the event of seeing as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once (it has not), without asserting a resulting state.

FullText: » You do not have his word dwelling in you . This is because you do not believe in the person he has sent .

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has sent' indicates a past action (sending) with a resulting state (the person being sent) that holds relevance at the reference time, encoding a transition from not-sent to sent state.

FullText: » Do not think that I will accuse you before the Father . Moses on whom you have set your hope is a plaintiff against you .

DtRes_explanation: The phrase 'have set your hope' uses the present perfect to indicate a completed action (setting hope) whose resulting state (hope being set on Moses) holds at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.

FullText: They gathered twelve baskets of leftovers after everyone had eaten .

ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had eaten' describes a completed event that actually occurred (people ate food, resulting in leftovers), not an absent or unrealized situation.

DtRes_explanation: The target 'had eaten' is past perfect indicating a prior completed action before another past event (gathering leftovers), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.

TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had eaten' presents the eating event as a bounded, completed whole prior to the gathering, with no internal temporal development accessible, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.

FullText: They boarded a boat to travel over the sea to Capernaum . It was dark and Jesus had not yet come to them .

DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has set his seal upon' presents the action as part of God's life history/actions, answering whether the sealing event has occurred at least once, without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has set his seal upon' indicates a completed action (God setting a seal) whose resulting state (the seal being set upon him) holds at the reference time, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has set his seal upon' expresses a completed past action with present relevance (present perfect), not a continuous state or activity holding from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has set his seal upon' presents the action of sealing as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has set his seal upon him' is asserted as a realized fact about God's action, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

FullText: Jesus answered : » This is the work of God , that you have an active faith in the one whom he has sent . «

DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has sent' indicates a past action (God sending someone) with a resulting state that holds at the reference time (the one whom God sent exists/is present), encoding a transition from State A (not sent) to State B (sent and currently in effect).
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has sent' expresses a completed past action (perfect aspect) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has sent' presents the sending as a completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition as a bounded whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has sent' is asserted as a realized fact about God's action in the actual world, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.

FullText: » I told you . You have seen me and yet do not believe .

DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' presents the event as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.

FullText: » This is the will of him that sent me that I should not lose any of those he has given me , but I should raise them up at the last day .

ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'agree' in 'had agreed' encodes a two-participant mental/speech act event where both participants (the Jews as collective agent and the implicit content of agreement) are relatively symmetric in their relationship, rather than one participant being directed toward and affected by another in an asymmetric transitive manner.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'agreed' encodes a two-participant event requiring an external argument (the Jews as agent) and an internal argument (the proposition), thus it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'agreed' licenses an internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Had agreed is a stative perfect expressing a completed state of agreement rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: Had agreed describes a prior agreement or consensus state rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it represents the resultant state of a past decision process, not the boundary point of transition itself.
AktAct_explanation: Had agreed is a perfective past action with a defined endpoint (the agreement was reached) and result state (an agreement exists), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Had agreed describes a resultant state of mutual commitment rather than a punctual dynamic event, as it entails a consequent condition (the agreement to put someone out of the synagogue).
AktState_explanation: The target 'had agreed' describes a completed action of reaching consensus, which involves dynamic change and has an inherent endpoint, thus does not instantiate AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue' describes a factual agreement that actually occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had agreed' presents a realized event (the Jews did agree on a rule), not an absent or non-occurring situation, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause containing 'had agreed' lacks an overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' presents a past event as part of the Jews' life history, answering whether an agreement occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' indicates a change of state from non-agreement to agreement, with the resulting state (the Jews being in agreement) holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' expresses a past state (the agreement) that holds as a completed condition relevant to the reference time of the narrative, indicating a continuous state from a past starting point up to the reference time of the main clause.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The form 'had agreed' presents the agreement as a completed, bounded event viewed as a whole without internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had agreed' is asserted as a realized fact about what the Jews actually did, not as a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had agreed' is a past perfect form (had + past participle), which encodes a tense specification (past) other than the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had agreed' is an active voice construction where 'The Jews' is the grammatical subject and semantic agent performing the action of agreeing, not a patient receiving the action.

FullText: » No one takes it away from me , but I lay it down voluntarily . I have authority jurisdiction right to lay it down , and I have authority to take it again . I have received this commandment from my Father . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'received' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (I/agent) and an internal argument (this commandment/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the external argument toward and affecting the internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have received' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (I/subject) and an internal argument (this commandment/object), thus does not instantiate the one-participant ASUncc feature.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'received' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('this commandment'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires a one-participant event with no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' describes a completed past action with present relevance, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase unfolding toward a terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: Have received describes the resultant state of a past reception event rather than the punctual threshold-crossing moment itself, making it stative rather than an achievement (AktAch).
AktAct_explanation: Have received is a perfective aspect denoting a completed action with a resultant state (possession of the commandment), not a dynamic durative situation unfolding without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have received describes a completed action with a resulting state (possession of the commandment), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' describes a completed action with a defined endpoint in the past, not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have received this commandment from my Father' describes a factual event that is presented as having actually occurred, not a counterfactual or contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have received' presents the event as actually occurring and realized in reality, not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have received this commandment from my Father' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' presents the receiving of the commandment as an event that occurred in the past and is part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' indicates a past action (receiving the commandment) with a resulting state (currently possessing/holding that commandment) that persists at the reference time, instantiating the DtRes feature.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' expresses a completed past action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, failing to meet the DtUniv criterion of continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have received' presents the receiving of the commandment as a completed, bounded event with relevance to the present moment, treating the transition of receiving as a single whole without internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have received' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (the speaker states a completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have received' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have received' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent performing the action of receiving, not a patient; there is no overt passive morphology and the agent is not expressed in a by-phrase.

FullText: » My Father has given them to me . He is greater than all and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father’s hand .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'give' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (Father as agent/initiator) and an internal argument (them as patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the agent toward and affecting the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' encodes a three-participant event (giver, recipient, theme) with an external argument (My Father), so it does not instantiate ASUncc which requires a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses two internal arguments ('them' and 'to me'), violating the requirement that no internal argument be licensed for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' describes a completed action with a defined terminal point (the transfer of possession) that resulted in a current state (them being with me), instantiating the AktAcc feature of a telic event with inherent culmination.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it represents a prior action whose effects persist, not the crossing of a threshold boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' describes a completed action with a result state (them being given to me), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' describes a completed action with a resulting state (them now being possessed by the speaker), which entails a change of state and thus does not instantiate AktSemel, which requires no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' describes a completed dynamic action with a resultant state, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'My Father has given them to me' describes a factual event that is presented as having actually occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has given' presents a completed action as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or non-occurring, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'My Father has given them to me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' presents the giving event as part of the Father's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' encodes a transition from a state where the Father had not given the objects to a state where He has given them to the speaker, with this resulting state of possession currently holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' expresses a past action (the giving) with present relevance, indicating a state that holds from that past point to the reference time (the Father's possession and bestowal of them remains in effect).
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has given' presents the giving as a completed action with a bounded endpoint, construing the transition of transfer as a single whole event without accessing its internal temporal development.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has given' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (present perfect indicating a completed action), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has given' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'given') with no additional tense, mood, or aspect marking, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' is active voice with the agent (Father) as grammatical subject and the semantic patient (them) as object, lacking passive morphological marking and by-phrase structure required for VcPass.

FullText: Jesus replied to them : » I have shown you many good works from the Father . For which of those works do you stone me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'shown' encodes a two-participant event with Jesus as external argument (agent/initiator) and 'you' as internal argument (patient/theme), with the event construed as directed from Jesus toward the recipients and affecting their knowledge/perception.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have shown' licenses two arguments (external: 'I', internal: 'you' and 'many good works'), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have shown' licenses two internal arguments ('you' as indirect object and 'many good works' as direct object), violating the ASUnrg requirement that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' denotes a dynamic action of demonstrating works that unfolds over time with a defined result state (the works now being visible/known to the audience), instantiating AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold; it represents an unfolding process of demonstrating works, not a boundary point of transition.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' describes a completed action with a resultant state (works that have been demonstrated), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have shown entails a resulting change of state (the works are now known/visible to the audience), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' describes a completed action with results relevant to the present moment, involving dynamic change and progression from a state of not having shown to having shown, which contradicts the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have shown you many good works' describes an actual event that has occurred in the world of the text, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have shown' presents the event as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'many good works' is a direct object, not a temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' presents the action as part of Jesus's life history, answering whether the event of showing good works has occurred at least once, without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' indicates a change of state from not having demonstrated the works to having demonstrated them, with the resulting state (the works being shown) holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' expresses a state of completed actions (displaying good works) that extends from a past point up to the reference time of speaking, instantiating the DtUniv feature.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have shown' presents the action of showing as a completed event with results relevant to the present moment, construing the action as a bounded whole without internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have shown' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus claims to have performed good works), not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have shown' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have shown' is active voice with the agent (I/Jesus) as grammatical subject and the patient (you/many good works) as object, not passive voice.

FullText: Then he told them : » Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep . I go to wake him out of sleep . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'fall asleep' is an unaccusative intransitive verb with only an internal argument (the subject undergoing the state change), lacking the two-participant transitive structure with an external agent directing an action toward an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' encodes a two-participant event where the subject 'he' (Lazarus) functions as an external argument (agent/experiencer), not solely as an internal argument, thus does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' is a two-participant event where the subject (Lazarus) is the experiencer/patient undergoing a state change, not an agent/initiator controlling the action, and the construction licenses an internal argument (the complement 'asleep'), violating the one-participant agent-only requirement.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has fallen asleep' describes a dynamic event (falling) that unfolds over time and culminates in a defined terminal point (the state of being asleep), with an inherent process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has fallen asleep' instantiates AktAch as it represents a punctual change-of-state event marking the boundary transition from being awake to being asleep, conceived as a near-instantaneous crossing of a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: Has fallen asleep describes a completed change of state with an inherent endpoint (the transition to sleep), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Has fallen asleep entails a resulting change of state (from awake to asleep) and is not punctual/instantaneous, thus does not instantiate AktSemel which requires no resulting state change.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has fallen asleep' describes a dynamic change from being awake to being asleep, with an inherent endpoint (the moment sleep was achieved), which contradicts the stative nature of AktState that requires no dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has fallen asleep' describes an actual event that has occurred in the world of the text, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' presents an event that has occurred and is realized in reality, not absent or negated; the speaker affirms that Lazarus is in a state of sleep.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'Then' modifies the reporting clause, not the target clause itself.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'has fallen asleep' presents the event as part of Lazarus's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once, without asserting that a resulting state (being asleep) currently holds in the speaker's intended meaning.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'has fallen asleep' encodes a transition from the state of being awake to the state of being asleep, with the resulting state (asleep) holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' describes a completed action with present relevance (the state of being asleep now), not a continuous state or activity holding from a past starting point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has fallen asleep' presents the transition into sleep as a completed, bounded event without exposing its internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the speaker's statement, not construed as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'fallen') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has fallen asleep' is active voice with the semantic agent (Lazarus) as the grammatical subject, lacking the passive voice morphological marking and by-phrase structure required for VcPass.

FullText: The disciples said , » Lord , if he has fallen asleep , he would recover . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' is an intransitive stative construction with only one participant (the subject 'he'), lacking the two-participant transitive structure with an external agent directing an action toward an affected internal argument required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' is a two-participant event where 'he' functions as an external argument (agent/experiencer) controlling the state, not solely as an internal argument/theme, thus does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' encodes a two-participant event where 'he' is the internal argument (experiencer/patient undergoing the state change) rather than an external agent/initiator, violating the one-participant agent-only requirement of ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'has fallen asleep' describes a dynamic event with a process phase (the act of falling) that culminates in a defined result state (being asleep), instantiating the AktAcc feature.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'has fallen asleep' instantiates AktAch as it represents a punctual change-of-state event marking the boundary transition from being awake to being asleep, conceived as a threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' describes a completed change of state with an inherent endpoint (the transition to sleep), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' entails a resulting change of state (from awake to asleep) and is not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' describes a dynamic change from wakefulness to sleep with an inherent endpoint (the moment sleep is achieved), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has fallen asleep' presents a hypothetical condition (introduced by 'if') that the disciples are considering as a possibility, not as contrary to fact; the counterfactual element lies in the consequent 'he would recover,' not in this target phrase itself.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' presents the event as a realized condition (the disciples are discussing whether he has actually fallen asleep), not as absent from reality or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has fallen asleep' is embedded in a conditional clause ('if he has fallen asleep') that expresses a hypothetical scenario rather than asserting an event as part of the subject's actual life history.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has fallen asleep' encodes a transition from the state of being awake to the state of being asleep, with the resulting state (asleep) holding at the reference time, thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has fallen asleep' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a continuous state from past to present, so it does not instantiate DtUniv which requires continuous holding from past starting point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has fallen asleep' presents the transition to sleep as a completed, bounded event without accessing its internal temporal development, instantiating the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' is asserted as a realized fact within the disciples' conditional statement, not construed as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has fallen asleep' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'fallen') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has fallen asleep' is an active voice periphrastic form where the subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the action of falling asleep, not the patient; there is no passive morphological marking or by-phrase present.

FullText: When Jesus arrived he found Lazarus had been in the grave four days .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' is a stative passive construction describing a resultant state rather than a transitive event with an agent directing action toward a patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been' is an auxiliary construction with the lexical verb 'be', which encodes a two-participant stative relation (subject and locative complement) rather than a one-participant event with an internal argument, and it licenses an external argument (the subject 'Lazarus').
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been' is a passive perfect auxiliary construction describing a state (being in the grave), not a one-participant event with an external agent argument; Lazarus is the experiencer/theme, not an agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' describes a stative condition (being in a location) rather than a dynamic event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' expresses a stative condition of remaining in a location over four days, not a punctual change-of-state event or threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' describes a stative condition with a defined endpoint (four days), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement that can be interrupted without implying failure.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' is a stative perfect construction describing a resulting state of being in the grave for four days, not a dynamic punctual event without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' describes a stative situation (being in the grave) that holds over a duration (four days) without dynamic change or inherent endpoint, where any subinterval is qualitatively identical to the whole.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target describes a factual state that actually occurred (Lazarus was in the grave for four days), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' describes a realized state of affairs (Lazarus actually was in the grave for four days), not an absent or non-occurring event, so it does not instantiate ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the overt temporal adverbial phrase 'four days' which explicitly anchors the event of being in the grave to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' presents a resulting state (Lazarus remaining in the grave for four days) rather than a life-history event, focusing on duration and current condition rather than whether the event occurred.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' describes a state that was already in effect before the reference time (when Jesus arrived), rather than encoding a transition from State A to State B with the new state currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' expresses a state (being in the grave) that held continuously from a past starting point (when Lazarus died) up to the reference time (when Jesus arrived), matching the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' presents a state of being in a location over a duration (four days) rather than a bounded transition or completed event, thus does not instantiate perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been (in the grave)' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (Lazarus was actually in the grave for four days), not as projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been' is a stative construction describing location/condition, not a passive voice construction; there is no semantic patient functioning as grammatical subject, no agent expressed or omitted, and no overt passive morphology (would require past participle like 'had been buried').

FullText: Martha said to Jesus : » Lord if you had been here my brother would not have died .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative locative construction expressing presence/location, not a transitive event with an external argument directing action toward an affected internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'been' in 'had been here' is a stative copula that licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'you'), not a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a locative copular construction with 'been' (copula) and 'here' (locational predicate), not a one-participant event with an external argument agent/initiator; it encodes a stative location rather than an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative past perfect construction expressing a hypothetical state of presence, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative past perfect construction expressing a hypothetical condition, not a dynamic change-of-state event or punctual threshold crossing.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been here' expresses a counterfactual conditional state with an implied endpoint (Jesus's presence would have prevented death), not a dynamic durative situation unfolding without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative perfect construction expressing a resultant state (presence at a location), not a dynamic punctual event without resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been here' describes a hypothetical past state of presence that would have involved dynamic change (arrival) and would have had consequences, failing to meet the criterion of a situation holding without dynamic change or inherent progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'if you had been here' presents a counterfactual condition—it expresses that Jesus was not actually present, while implying that if he had been, the brother would not have died.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized/absent state—Jesus was not actually present at that location, and the gram construes this absence from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had been here' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'here' is a locative adverbial, not a temporal one.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional (contrary-to-fact) expressing what did not happen, not a past event presented as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional in past perfect form expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized past state, not a continuous state holding from a past point to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect progressive expressing a hypothetical state in a counterfactual conditional, not a bounded completed event; it presents internal temporal development (duration of being present) rather than a transition as a single whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'had been here' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('if you had been here...would not have died') that expresses a hypothetical, non-actual situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been here' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, and appears in a counterfactual conditional construction.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect form of the stative verb 'be' expressing location, not a passive construction; it lacks the semantic patient-as-subject structure, agent in by-phrase, or passive morphological marking required for VcPass.

FullText: Martha said to Jesus : » Lord if you had been here my brother would not have died .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'die' is intransitive and encodes a one-participant event (the subject undergoing a state change) rather than a two-participant transitive event with an external argument directing action toward an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'died' encodes a one-participant event with an internal argument, but the conditional construction 'would not have died' licenses an implicit external argument (the brother as agent/experiencer of the dying), violating the requirement that the verb not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'died' is a one-participant event, but the sole argument 'brother' functions as an internal argument (patient/theme undergoing the event), not as an external argument (agent/initiator), so ASUnrg is not instantiated.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional in the past tense expressing a negated state rather than a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined result.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized state rather than a punctual change-of-state event, lacking the dynamic threshold-crossing quality required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would not have died' describes a resultative state (non-death) with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have died' entails a resulting change of state (from living to dead), which contradicts the AktSemel feature requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would not have died' describes a dynamic change (transition from life to death) with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is the consequent of a counterfactual conditional ('if you had been here') expressing a contrary-to-fact situation: the brother did die, and Jesus was not there, making the stated outcome contrary to actual fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would not have died' presents the event of dying as not occurring—it construes the brother's death as absent from the counterfactual reality described in the conditional statement.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'would not have died' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure indicates hypothetical pastness but lacks an overt temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different circumstances, not a statement about whether an event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target gram 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under unrealized conditions, not an actual change of state that has occurred with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened in an unrealized past scenario, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical situation, not a bounded completed event; it lacks the perfective aspect's characteristic of presenting a situation as a bounded whole.

FullText: When Mary saw Jesus she fell down at his feet . She said : » Lord ; if you had been here my brother would not have died . «
