ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had been' licenses an external argument (you) functioning as the subject/agent, not an internal argument, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula) with locative complement 'here' licenses an internal argument (the location), violating the one-participant external-argument-only requirement of ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative past perfect construction expressing a hypothetical condition, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect progressive expressing a hypothetical state of presence, not a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect stative construction expressing a completed state of presence, not a dynamic durative situation unfolding over time without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a stative past perfect construction expressing a resulting state of presence, not a dynamic punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been here' expresses a counterfactual conditional state that involves dynamic change (Jesus's presence/absence would have altered the outcome), not a static situation holding uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'if you had been here' presents a counterfactual condition: Jesus was not actually present at that time, and Mary expresses that if he had been (contrary to fact), her brother would not have died.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had been here' presents a counterfactual condition (Jesus not being present at that time), construing the event as not having occurred in reality, which instantiates ClNegPol.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'had been here' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure expresses a counterfactual rather than a definite temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not a past event presented as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized past state, not an actual change of state with a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been here' expresses a continuous state (presence at a location) that would have held from a past starting point up to the reference time of the brother's death, fitting the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect progressive expressing a counterfactual condition, presenting an unbounded state of presence rather than a bounded completed event, thus failing to instantiate TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been here' is part of a counterfactual conditional ('if you had been here...my brother would not have died') that construes a non-actual, hypothetical situation contrary to what actually occurred.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been here' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect (pluperfect) tense, which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect, and appears in a counterfactual conditional structure.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been here' is a past perfect copular construction expressing location, not a passive voice construction; it lacks a semantic patient as grammatical subject and overt passive morphology.

FullText: When Mary saw Jesus she fell down at his feet . She said : » Lord ; if you had been here my brother would not have died . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'die' is intransitive and encodes a one-participant event (the subject undergoing a state change) rather than a two-participant transitive event with an external argument directing action toward an internal argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'died' encodes a one-participant event with an internal argument, but the conditional construction 'would not have died' licenses an implicit external argument (the brother as agent/experiencer of the dying event), violating the requirement that the verb not license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'died' is intransitive and takes only one participant (the brother as external argument), but the feature ASUnrg requires the sole argument to function as an agent/initiator; 'died' encodes a non-volitional state change where the argument is a patient/undergoer, not an agent.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical non-occurrence of death, lacking the dynamic durative process with inherent culmination that characterizes AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would not have died' instantiates AktAch as it represents a punctual change-of-state event (the crossing of the threshold from life to death) conceived as a boundary point of transition, even though expressed in counterfactual conditional form.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would not have died' describes a resultative state (non-death) with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would not have died' entails a resulting change of state (from living to dead), which contradicts the AktSemel feature requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would not have died' describes a dynamic change (death as a transition from life to non-life) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'would not have died' is part of a counterfactual conditional ('if you had been here my brother would not have died') that presents a contrary-to-fact situation: the brother actually died, but Mary expresses what would not have happened under different conditions (if Jesus had been present).
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'would not have died' presents the event of the brother's death as not occurring under the hypothetical condition, construing the situation as absent from the realized reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would not have died' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the conditional structure indicates counterfactual meaning but lacks the required temporal adverbial anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing what would have happened under different circumstances, not an assertion that the event occurred as part of the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized hypothetical state, not an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would not have died' expresses a counterfactual conditional consequence (what would have happened if conditions were different), not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The construction 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional in the apodosis expressing an unrealized, hypothetical situation rather than a bounded completed event, thus does not instantiate perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical, non-actual situation dependent on an unfulfilled condition ('if you had been here'), thus instantiating TLMdIrr.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would not have died' instantiates TLTS because it encodes modal specification (would) combined with perfect aspect (have + past participle), which is a non-canonical perfect form distinct from the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have died' is an active voice construction where 'my brother' (the semantic patient) functions as the grammatical subject, but there is no overt passive morphological marking (no 'be' auxiliary + past participle form characteristic of passive voice); 'died' is a simple past participle used in active voice with modal and perfect aspect.

FullText: He asked : » Where have you laid him ? « They answered : » Come and see Lord . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'laid' encodes a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (agent 'you') directing an action toward an internal argument (patient 'him'), where the action affects the location/state of the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'laid' is transitive and licenses an external argument (the understood subject 'you'), with 'him' as the internal argument, so it does not instantiate a one-participant event where the sole argument is internal.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'laid' licenses an internal argument ('him'), so it does not encode a one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' describes a completed action (placing a body) with a defined terminal point and result state (the body now being in a specific location), instantiating the AktAcc feature of a telic, culminating event.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' describes a completed action with present relevance rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it represents the result state of placing/positioning rather than the instantaneous threshold crossing itself.
AktAct_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' describes a completed action with a resultant state (the location where someone has been placed), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have laid entails a resulting change of state (the object is now in a laid position), which violates the AktSemel requirement of no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' describes a completed action with current relevance (the placement of a body), which involves dynamic change and has an inherent endpoint, thus does not instantiate AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'have you laid him' is a present perfect question asking about a completed action in the actual world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'have laid' is a present perfect form describing a completed action (where they placed him), not negating or presenting the event as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'have laid' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' presents the action as part of the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' indicates a completed action (laying him somewhere) whose resulting state (him being in that location) holds at the reference time of the question, instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' expresses a completed past action with present relevance (where the body currently is), not a state or activity holding continuously from past to present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'have laid' presents the action of laying as a completed event with relevance to the present moment, construing the transition as a bounded whole without exposing internal temporal development, which instantiates the perfective aspect feature TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have laid' is used in a direct question about a completed action in the actual world (where Jesus's body was placed), not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have laid' is a canonical present perfect form (have + past participle) with no overt encoding of tense or mood specifications beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have laid' is active voice with 'you' as the grammatical subject performing the action of laying, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: Some of them said : » This man opened the eyes of the blind . Could he have prevented this man from dying ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'prevented' encodes a two-participant event with an external argument (the man, implicit agent) and an internal argument (the man from dying, patient/theme), where the event is construed as directed from the agent toward and affecting the patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'prevented' encodes a two-participant event (preventer and prevented entity) with an external argument (the implicit agent 'he') and does not match the one-participant internal-argument-only profile required by ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'prevented' licenses two internal arguments (the infinitival complement 'from dying' and the object 'this man'), making it a two-participant event that does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'have prevented (from dying)' instantiates AktAcc as it describes a dynamic, durative event with an inherent process (the act of preventing) that necessarily culminates in a defined terminal point (the result state of death being averted).
AktAch_explanation: The target 'have prevented (from dying)' describes a counterfactual ability to stop an ongoing process rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have prevented (from dying)' expresses a perfective, telic action with an inherent endpoint (prevention of death as a defined goal), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Have prevented (from dying) entails a resulting change of state (the man remaining alive rather than dead), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'have prevented (from dying)' describes a dynamic change with an inherent endpoint (preventing death), not a state that holds uniformly over time without progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Could he have prevented this man from dying?' presents a counterfactual condition—it questions whether an alternative action (prevention of death) could have occurred under different circumstances, implying the man did in fact die and was not prevented from doing so.
ClNegPol_explanation: The conditional question 'Could he have prevented this man from dying?' presents the prevention as a hypothetical unrealized event, construing it as absent from reality rather than as something that actually occurred.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the modal 'could' expresses hypothetical possibility rather than temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'have prevented' appears in a counterfactual conditional question ('Could he have prevented...?') that does not assert the event occurred in the subject's life history, but rather poses a hypothetical possibility.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'have prevented (from dying)' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical possibility rather than an actual change of state that has occurred and holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'have prevented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical possibility, not a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional modal construction 'Could he have prevented' presents a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than a bounded completed event, failing to meet the perfective aspect requirement of presenting the situation as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'have prevented' appears in a rhetorical question ('Could he have prevented this man from dying?') that construes the situation as hypothetical and non-actual rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have prevented' is a modal perfect form (could have + past participle), which encodes modal specification beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have prevented' is in active voice with 'he' as the grammatical subject (the agent), not passive voice where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: Jesus said : » Take away the stone . « Martha , the sister of the dead man said : » Lord by now the body has decayed for he has been dead four days . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'decay' is an intransitive process verb where the body (internal argument) undergoes spontaneous decomposition without an external agent directing the action toward it, failing to meet the two-participant transitive event structure required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'decay' in 'has decayed' licenses an external argument (the body as agent/experiencer undergoing the decay process), not solely an internal argument, and describes a change-of-state event with an implicit external participant.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'decay' in 'has decayed' is a one-participant event where the body functions as the internal argument (patient/theme) undergoing the decay process, not as an external argument/agent, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'has decayed' describes a state resulting from a completed process rather than a dynamic event unfolding toward a terminal point, lacking the inherent process phase leading toward completion required by AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: Has decayed describes a completed state of decomposition rather than the punctual moment of transition into decay, focusing on the resulting condition after an extended process rather than the threshold-crossing event itself.
AktAct_explanation: Has decayed describes a completed state change with an inherent endpoint (the body reaching a decayed state), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: Has decayed describes a resultant state (the body is in a decayed condition) rather than a punctual event without resulting change, as it entails a consequent result condition of decomposition.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has decayed' describes a dynamic change process with an inherent endpoint (the completion of decay), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the body has decayed' describes an actual state of affairs in the world of the narrative (the body has actually decayed after four days), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has decayed' describes an event that has actually occurred and is realized in reality (the body's decomposition is presented as a factual state), not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the overt temporal adverbial 'by now' which explicitly anchors the event of decay to a specific past time interval (the present moment relative to the four-day period since death).
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'has decayed' asserts a resulting state (the body is currently in a decayed condition) rather than merely recording whether the event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'has decayed' indicates a change of state from non-decayed to decayed, with the resulting state (decay) holding at the reference time of the utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The present perfect 'has decayed' expresses a state (decay) that began in the past (when the man died four days ago) and continues up to the reference time (the moment of speech), instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The present perfect 'has decayed' presents the decay as a completed state resulting from a bounded transition, making the internal temporal development inaccessible and targeting the transition itself as a single accomplished event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'has decayed' is asserted as a realized fact about the actual state of the dead body, not as a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has decayed' is a canonical present perfect form (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'decayed') with no overt tense or mood specification beyond the simple present perfect, so TLTS is not instantiated.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has decayed' is an active voice construction where the semantic patient (the body) functions as the grammatical subject, but there is no overt passive morphological marking (no 'been' auxiliary before the past participle) and no agent expression, making it active rather than passive.

FullText: Jesus said : » Take away the stone . « Martha , the sister of the dead man said : » Lord by now the body has decayed for he has been dead four days . «

ASUncc_explanation: The target 'has been dead' is a stative predicate where 'dead' is an adjective functioning as a complement; it does not encode a one-participant event with an internal argument theme/patient, and the subject 'he' functions as an external argument (experiencer/theme of state), not as an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'dead' is a stative adjective (not a dynamic lexical verb) that takes an internal argument (the subject 'he'), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with only an external argument and no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'has been dead' describes a stative condition (being in a state of death) rather than a dynamic event with an inherent process phase culminating in a defined terminal point, thus it does not instantiate AktAcc.

AktAct_explanation: The target 'has been dead' describes a resultant state (death) with an inherent endpoint (the moment of death), not a dynamic durative situation requiring sustained engagement that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Has been dead is a stative predicate expressing a resulting state of death rather than a dynamic punctual event, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'has been dead' describes a state that holds over time without dynamic change or inherent progression—death is a static condition where any subinterval (one day dead, two days dead, four days dead) is qualitatively identical to the whole.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target 'has been dead' describes an actual fact in the narrative world (the man is actually dead for four days), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'has been dead' describes an actual state that is realized and holding in reality (the man is actually dead), not an absent or non-occurring event.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target 'has been dead' contains the overt temporal adverbial 'four days' which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'has been dead' asserts a resulting state (the man is currently dead) rather than merely recording whether the event occurred in the subject's life history, failing to match the DtExp feature definition.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'has been dead' indicates a change of state from alive to dead with the resulting state (being dead) holding at the reference time (four days after death), thus instantiating DtRes.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'has been dead' expresses a state that began in the past (when the man died) and continues up to the reference time (the present moment of Martha's speech), matching the DtUniv feature definition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The target 'has been dead' presents a state (deadness) as ongoing from a past point to the present moment, making internal temporal development accessible through the perfect aspect, rather than bounding the situation as a completed whole event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has been dead' asserts a realized fact about the past state of the dead man, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has been dead' instantiates TLTS because it encodes a progressive perfect form ('has been' + adjective functioning as state descriptor) rather than the simple present perfect, thus specifying a tense/mood specification beyond the canonical 'have/has' + past participle structure.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been dead' is a stative adjective construction, not a passive voice construction; it lacks the semantic patient-as-subject structure and overt passive morphology required for VcPass.

FullText: Some of them went to the Pharisees and told them the things that Jesus had done .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' encodes a two-participant transitive event with Jesus as the external argument (agent) and 'things' as the internal argument (patient/theme) that is affected by Jesus's actions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' in 'had done' is a two-participant event where Jesus (external argument/agent) performs actions on 'things' (internal argument/theme), so it licenses an external argument and does not meet the one-participant criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument (the things that Jesus had done), so it does not instantiate a one-participant event with only an external argument.

AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' describes a completed action viewed as a whole rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a threshold; it refers to Jesus's miracles as an accomplished fact, not a dynamic transition point.

AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had done' describes a completed action with a resulting change of state (Jesus performed miracles/actions that produced effects), not a punctual event without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had done' describes completed dynamic actions (the things Jesus performed), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'that Jesus had done' reports actual events that occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had done' presents the event of Jesus performing actions as realized and occurring in reality, not as absent or negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'had done' is a past perfect verb form but lacks an accompanying temporal adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had done' presents Jesus's actions as part of his life history, answering whether the events occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had done' is past perfect indicating a prior completed action relative to the narrative past ('told'), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had done' expresses a completed past action (past perfect) rather than a state or activity holding continuously from a past point to the reference time, so it does not instantiate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents Jesus's actions as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, consistent with perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had done' reports a realized fact (Jesus's actions) that occurred in the actual world, not a projected, hypothetical, imagined, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had done' is a past perfect form that encodes tense specification (past) beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice with Jesus as the grammatical subject and agent performing the action, not a passive construction where the patient would be the subject.

FullText: Now the chief priests and the Pharisees had given orders that , if any man knew where he was , he should report it that they might seize him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'give' in 'had given orders' encodes a two-participant event with the chief priests and Pharisees as external argument (agents initiating the action) and 'orders' as internal argument (patient/theme affected by the giving), construed as directed from the agents toward the orders.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'given' encodes a two-participant event (chief priests/Pharisees as external argument giving orders as internal argument), not a one-participant event with sole internal argument, so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'give' in 'had given orders' licenses two internal arguments (orders as direct object and an implicit recipient), violating the one-participant, no-internal-argument requirement of ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'had given orders' is a perfective past action with a defined completion point but lacks the durative process phase unfolding over time that characterizes AktAcc; it represents a single completed speech act rather than a dynamic event necessarily culminating through an inherent process.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had given orders' describes a completed action of issuing instructions rather than a punctual change-of-state event crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had given orders' describes a completed action with a defined result state (orders were issued), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had given orders' entails a resulting change of state (orders are now in effect with consequent obligations), which disqualifies it from AktSemel, which requires no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had given orders' describes a completed action with a definite endpoint (the issuing of orders), not a state that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had given orders' describes a factual past event that actually occurred (the chief priests and Pharisees did issue these orders), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had given orders' presents an event that actually occurred and was realized in reality, not an absent or non-occurring situation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'Now' is a discourse marker, not a temporal anchor for the event itself.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'had given orders' presents the event of giving orders as part of the chief priests' and Pharisees' life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The gram 'had given orders' encodes a change of state from a prior condition (no orders) to a resulting state (orders now in effect), with the new state holding at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had given orders' expresses a past action (giving orders) that establishes an ongoing state or directive extending from that past point to the reference time of the narrative, instantiating DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had given orders' presents the act of issuing orders as a completed, bounded event with no internal temporal development accessible, treating the transition of giving orders as a single finished whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had given orders' asserts a realized fact (the orders were actually given in the past), not a projected or hypothetical situation, so it does not instantiate TLMdIrr.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had given orders' instantiates TLTS because it encodes past perfect tense (pluperfect), which is a tense specification other than the simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had given orders' is active voice with the chief priests and Pharisees as the grammatical subject performing the action of giving, not a passive construction where the semantic patient functions as the grammatical subject.

FullText: His disciples did not understand these things at first . However when Jesus was glorified they remembered the things that were written about him . They also remembered the things that had been done to him .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done to him' encodes a two-participant event where an implicit external argument (agent) directs an action toward the internal argument (him/patient), satisfying the ASTrns feature requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' in the passive construction 'had been done to him' encodes a one-participant event where the sole argument 'him' functions as an internal argument (patient/theme), and the verb does not license an external argument in the surface structure.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction with an internal argument ('to him'), violating the requirement for a one-participant event with only an external argument and no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'had been done to him' describes a dynamic, durative event (actions performed on Jesus) that unfolded over time and culminated in a defined result state (completion of those actions), satisfying the AktAcc feature criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed state or process rather than a punctual change-of-state event; it does not represent the boundary point of transition itself but rather the resultant state after actions were performed.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'had been done to him' describes completed actions with inherent endpoints (things that happened to Jesus), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a resulting state of completion with consequent effects (things that were done to Jesus), not a punctual event without resulting change of state, thus does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes completed actions with inherent endpoints (things that happened to Jesus), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'had been done to him' describes actual events that occurred in the past (things that were actually done to Jesus), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes events that actually occurred and were remembered; it does not present the situation as absent from reality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'that had been done to him' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'that' is a relative pronoun, not an adverbial anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The target 'had been done' presents events as part of the subject's (Jesus's) life history, answering whether things occurred to him without asserting a current resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a past completed action relative to the reference time (when they remembered), not a change of state with a resulting state holding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes a completed past action (things that were done to Jesus), not a state or activity that holds continuously from a past point up to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The form 'had been done' presents the actions performed to Jesus as a bounded, completed whole without internal temporal development, instantiating perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'had been done' describes past events that were realized facts (things actually done to Jesus), not projected or hypothetical situations.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a past perfect passive form, which encodes past tense specification beyond the simple present perfect, thus instantiating the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' exhibits overt passive morphology (auxiliary 'been' + past participle 'done'), the semantic patient 'things' functions as grammatical subject, and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for VcPass.
